topics.title: Measurement of temperature using Optical Pyrometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL508 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION & IOT LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of temperature using Optical Pyrometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Standard Definition**
> An **Optical Pyrometer** is a non-contact temperature measuring instrument that determines the temperature of a hot body by **comparing the brightness (luminous intensity) of the monochromatic radiation emitted by the target body** with that of a **calibrated reference filament** housed inside the instrument. The most widely used variant is the **Disappearing Filament Optical Pyrometer (DFOP)**, which operates on the principle of **monochromatic brightness matching** and is governed by **Planck's Radiation Law** and **Wien's Approximation**.

**Key Terminology Table (KTU Glossary):**

| Term | Meaning | Engineering Relevance |
|------|---------|----------------------|
| Monochromatic Radiation | Radiation of a single wavelength (typically red, $\lambda \approx 0.65\ \mu m$) | Eliminates wavelength dependence in comparison |
| Brightness Temperature ($T_B$) | Apparent temperature of a body | Differs from true temperature for non-blackbodies |
| True Temperature ($T$) | Actual thermodynamic temperature | Computed from brightness + emissivity correction |
| Emissivity ($\epsilon$) | Ratio of emissive power to that of a blackbody | Critical for non-blackbody correction |
| Blackbody | Idealized perfect absorber/emitter ($\epsilon = 1$) | Calibration reference for all pyrometers |
| Second Radiation Constant ($C_2$) | $C_2 = \frac{hc}{k} = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\ m\cdot K$ | Universal constant used in pyrometric equations |

> [!NOTE] **Why "Disappearing" Filament?**
> When the brightness of the reference tungsten filament exactly matches the brightness of the target image, the filament becomes **invisible** (it blends into the target background). This null-detection principle is the heart of DFOP and provides a highly accurate, human-eye-independent reading.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing in a dark room trying to estimate how hot an electric stove coil is glowing. You instinctively compare its **red glow** with another reference light bulb in the room that you can dim or brighten using a knob. As you adjust the bulb, at one particular setting, the brightness of the bulb filament becomes **indistinguishable** from the stove coil's red glow — at that exact moment, both are equally bright, and you can read off the corresponding temperature from a calibrated dial.

**The Optical Pyrometer works exactly like this:**

1. The **stove coil** = the **unknown hot target** (furnace, molten metal, hot gas).
2. The **adjustable reference bulb** = the **calibrated tungsten filament** inside the pyrometer.
3. The **dimming knob** = the **variable rheostat** controlling the filament current.
4. The **calibrated dial** = the **temperature scale** graduated directly in °C or K.
5. The **moment of invisibility** = the **null point** where true temperature is read.

> [!TIP] **Mental Hook for Exams**
> "The hotter the object, the brighter its red glow — and the pyrometer simply asks: *how much electric current does it take to make my internal filament glow equally bright?* That current is calibrated directly to temperature."

---

### 1.3 Scope & Range of Optical Pyrometers

| Parameter | Typical Value | KTU Examination Note |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------|
| Temperature Range | $600°C$ to $6000°C$ | Cannot measure below ~600°C (insufficient visible glow) |
| Wavelength Used | $0.65\ \mu m$ (red) | Chosen in the visible region for eye-based comparison |
| Accuracy | $\pm 5°C$ to $\pm 20°C$ | Depends on calibration and emissivity |
| Object Distance | $0.5\ m$ to $\infty$ | No physical contact required |
| Response Time | $< 1$ second | Purely optical — instantaneous |

> [!WARNING] **Common Misconception**
> Optical pyrometers measure **brightness temperature**, NOT true temperature. For non-blackbodies, the true temperature $T$ is higher than brightness temperature $T_B$ and must be corrected using the **emissivity $\epsilon$** of the target material. Most KTU exam questions specifically test this distinction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Why Brightness Tells Us Temperature

Every object above absolute zero emits electromagnetic radiation. The intensity and spectral distribution of this radiation depend *only* on the object's temperature (for a blackbody) — this is the foundational **Kirchhoff's Law of Thermal Radiation**.

**The Physical Chain of Logic:**

1. **Hot Object → Electrons vibrate vigorously → Emit photons across the EM spectrum.**
2. **Higher temperature → More photons emitted per second → Higher radiant intensity (brightness).**
3. **At a fixed wavelength (monochromatic), brightness is a unique function of temperature.**
4. **Therefore, measuring brightness at a known wavelength is equivalent to measuring temperature.**

The mathematical description of this phenomenon comes from **Planck's Radiation Law**, which the optical pyrometer exploits.

---

### 2.2 Planck's Radiation Law (The Foundational Equation)

The spectral emissive power $E_{\lambda}$ of a blackbody at wavelength $\lambda$ and temperature $T$ is given by:

$$E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{C_2 / \lambda T} - 1}$$

**Where the constants are:**

$$C_1 = 2\pi h c^{2} = 3.7415 \times 10^{-16}\ W\cdot m^{2}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{h c}{k} = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\ m\cdot K$$

- $h$ = **Planck's constant** = $6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ J\cdot s$
- $c$ = **Speed of light** = $2.998 \times 10^{8}\ m/s$
- $k$ = **Boltzmann constant** = $1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ J/K$

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight**
> $C_2$ is the single most important constant in optical pyrometry. Every brightness-temperature relation is built around it. Always memorize: $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\ m\cdot K$.

---

### 2.3 Wien's Approximation (The Optical Pyrometer's Working Equation)

For the temperature and wavelength ranges of optical pyrometers:
- $T > 1000\ K$ (very hot)
- $\lambda \approx 0.65 \times 10^{-6}\ m$ (red)

The term $\lambda T$ is small, so $e^{C_2/\lambda T} \gg 1$, and the **$-1$ in Planck's equation can be neglected**. This yields **Wien's Approximation:**

$$E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) \approx \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2 / \lambda T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why Wien's Approximation Matters**
> The exponential form is *crucial* because it makes the **logarithm of brightness a linear function of $1/T$** — a property the optical pyrometer exploits for direct linear calibration.

---

### 2.4 The Fundamental Brightness Comparison Equation (KTU HIGH-YIELD)

When the optical pyrometer's filament (at temperature $T_f$) matches the brightness of the target (at temperature $T$):

$$E_{\lambda}(T_f) = E_{\lambda}(T)$$

Applying Wien's approximation to both sides and taking the natural logarithm:

$$\ln\left(\frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}}\right) - \frac{C_2}{\lambda T_f} = \ln\left(\frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}}\right) - \frac{C_2}{\lambda T}$$

The $C_1/\lambda^5$ terms cancel cleanly:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{T_f} - \frac{1}{T} = \frac{\lambda}{C_2} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{\epsilon_f}{\epsilon}\right)}$$

For a **blackbody** ($\epsilon = \epsilon_f = 1$):

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{T_f} = \frac{1}{T}}$$

> [!TIP] **Exam Gold**
> For blackbody targets, **brightness temperature equals true temperature**. The optical pyrometer's reading is **directly the true temperature** with no correction needed. This is why optical pyrometers are calibrated against standard blackbody furnaces.

---

### 2.5 Emissivity Correction (For Real Non-Blackbodies)

Most industrial targets (molten steel, tungsten, ceramics) are NOT blackbodies. For a real body with emissivity $\epsilon < 1$:

$$E_{\lambda}^{real} = \epsilon \cdot E_{\lambda}^{blackbody}$$

After matching filament to real target brightness, the pyrometer reads the **brightness temperature $T_B$**, where:

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_B} + \frac{\lambda}{C_2} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1}{\epsilon}\right)$$

Since $\epsilon < 1$, $\ln(1/\epsilon) > 0$, so $1/T > 1/T_B$, meaning **$T > T_B$** — the true temperature is always *higher* than the indicated brightness temperature for non-blackbodies.

---

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT] **All formulas below are HIGH-FREQUENCY in KTU Board Exams — memorize the table.**

| **#** | **Formula** | **Name / Use** | **Variables & Units** |
|-------|-------------|----------------|------------------------|
| 1 | $E_{\lambda} = \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}\left(e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1\right)}$ | Planck's Radiation Law | $E_{\lambda}$ in $W/m^{2}\cdot \mu m$; $T$ in $K$ |
| 2 | $E_{\lambda} \approx \dfrac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$ | Wien's Approximation (used in optical pyrometers) | Same as above |
| 3 | $C_2 = \dfrac{hc}{k} = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\ m\cdot K$ | Second Radiation Constant | Universal constant |
| 4 | $\lambda_{m} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}\ m\cdot K$ | Wien's Displacement Law | Peak emission wavelength |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{T_f} + \dfrac{\lambda}{C_2}\ln\left(\dfrac{1}{\epsilon}\right)$ | True temp from brightness temp | $T_f$ = filament temp, $\epsilon$ = emissivity |
| 6 | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ W/m^{2}K^{4}$ | Stefan-Boltzmann Constant (total radiation) | $E = \sigma T^{4}$ (blackbody) |
| 7 | $E = \epsilon \cdot \sigma T^{4}$ | Total Emissive Power (real body) | $\epsilon$ = emissivity of surface |
| 8 | $\dfrac{d \ln E_{\lambda}}{dT} = \dfrac{C_2}{\lambda T^{2}}$ | Sensitivity of pyrometer (rate of change of brightness with $T$) | Higher at small $\lambda$ |
| 9 | $b = \sigma T^{4}$ | Total Radiant Exitance | $b$ in $W/m^{2}$ |
| 10 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{\lambda T^{2}}{C_2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta E}{E}$ | Temperature Resolution of Optical Pyrometer | $\Delta E$ = minimum detectable brightness difference |

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight — Real-World Applications**
> - **Steel Industry**: Optical pyrometers measure molten steel in electric arc furnaces (1500-1800°C).
> - **Cement & Glass Kilns**: Continuous non-contact monitoring of rotary kiln interior.
> - **Aerospace**: Re-entry vehicle surface temperature measurement.
> - **Power Plants**: Boiler tube temperature monitoring in thermal power stations.
> - **Semiconductor**: Crystal growth furnace temperature in silicon ingot production.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Planck's Law to Wien's Approximation (The Heart of the Pyrometer Equation)

**Starting Point — Planck's Spectral Emissive Power:**

$$E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1}$$

**Step 1:** Define the dimensionless quantity $x = \frac{C_2}{\lambda T}$.

Then Planck's law becomes:

$$E_{\lambda} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{x} - 1}$$

**Step 2:** For an optical pyrometer operating at $\lambda = 0.65 \times 10^{-6}\ m$ and $T = 1000\ K$:

$$x = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{(0.65 \times 10^{-6})(1000)} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{6.5 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 22.14$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate $e^{x} = e^{22.14} \approx 4.13 \times 10^{9}$.

Since $e^{x} \gg 1$, we have $e^{x} - 1 \approx e^{x}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute this approximation back:

$$E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) \approx \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{C_2/\lambda T}}$$

**Step 5:** Bring the exponential to the numerator:

$$E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) \approx \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **This is the Wien's Approximation used by the optical pyrometer.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Brightness Comparison Equation (Examination Critical)

**Physical Setup:** The reference filament inside the pyrometer (at temperature $T_f$) has the same brightness as the target (at temperature $T$) at wavelength $\lambda$.

**Step 1:** Apply Wien's approximation to the **filament's brightness** (filament is approximated as a blackbody, $\epsilon_f = 1$):

$$E_{\lambda}^{filament} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T_f}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Wien's approximation to the **target's brightness** (with emissivity $\epsilon$):

$$E_{\lambda}^{target} = \epsilon \cdot \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$$

**Step 3:** Set them equal (null-point / disappearing filament condition):

$$\frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T_f} = \epsilon \cdot \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel the common factor $\frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}}$ from both sides:

$$e^{-C_2/\lambda T_f} = \epsilon \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$$

**Step 5:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-\frac{C_2}{\lambda T_f} = \ln(\epsilon) - \frac{C_2}{\lambda T}$$

**Step 6:** Rearrange to isolate $1/T$:

$$\frac{C_2}{\lambda T} = \frac{C_2}{\lambda T_f} + \ln(\epsilon)$$

**Step 7:** Multiply through by $\frac{\lambda}{C_2}$:

$$\boxed{\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_f} + \frac{\lambda}{C_2} \cdot \ln(\epsilon)}$$

**Step 8 (Blackbody case):** For $\epsilon = 1$, $\ln(1) = 0$:

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_f} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T = T_f$$

> [!TIP] **KTU Exam Trick**
> If a numerical problem gives you $T_f$, $\lambda$, and $\epsilon$, plug directly into the boxed equation. Convert $T$ to Kelvin first (always), then convert back to °C at the end.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Sensitivity Analysis (Why Red Light is Chosen)

**Question:** Why is the optical pyrometer designed to operate at the red end of the visible spectrum ($\lambda \approx 0.65\ \mu m$)?

**Step 1:** Take the natural logarithm of Wien's approximation:

$$\ln(E_{\lambda}) = \ln(C_1) - 5\ln(\lambda) - \frac{C_2}{\lambda T}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate both sides with respect to $T$:

$$\frac{1}{E_{\lambda}} \cdot \frac{dE_{\lambda}}{dT} = \frac{C_2}{\lambda T^{2}}$$

**Step 3:** The relative sensitivity is:

$$\frac{dE_{\lambda}/E_{\lambda}}{dT} = \frac{C_2}{\lambda T^{2}}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute numerical values for $\lambda = 0.65\ \mu m$ and $T = 2000\ K$:

$$\frac{dE_{\lambda}/E_{\lambda}}{dT} = \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{(0.65 \times 10^{-6})(2000)^{2}}$$

$$= \frac{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}}{2.6} \approx 5.53 \times 10^{-3}\ per\ K$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation — A **1 K change in temperature produces ~0.55% change in brightness**, which is comfortably detectable by the human eye. If we used a longer wavelength (e.g., infrared $5\ \mu m$), this sensitivity drops to $\sim 7.2 \times 10^{-5}\ per\ K$ — 77 times worse. Hence, **red light is optimal**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Trade-off**
> Even shorter wavelengths (e.g., UV) give higher sensitivity, but the human eye cannot see UV, defeating the purpose of an *optical* (eye-based) pyrometer. Red is the perfect compromise.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Calibration of Optical Pyrometer Using Standard Blackbody

**Calibration Principle:** The optical pyrometer is calibrated by sighting it into a **standard blackbody furnace** held at known temperatures (e.g., $800°C, 1000°C, 1200°C, 1400°C$).

**Step 1:** For each known blackbody temperature $T_i$, the pyrometer filament current $I_i$ is adjusted until the filament disappears.

**Step 2:** The corresponding filament temperature is $T_{f,i}$, and the resistance-temperature relationship of the tungsten filament gives:

$$T_{f,i} = f(I_i^{2} R_i)$$

**Step 3:** The pyrometer's dial is then marked so that for the current $I_i$ that produces $T_{f,i} = T_i$, the dial reads $T_i$.

**Step 4:** A **calibration curve of dial reading vs. true temperature** is plotted. The relationship is approximately linear in $1/T$ (from the Wien equation):

$$\frac{1}{T} \approx \frac{1}{T_f} = \frac{\lambda}{C_2} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{C_1 / \lambda^{5}}{E_{\lambda}}\right)$$

A plot of $1/T$ vs. $\ln(E_{\lambda})$ yields a straight line of slope $\lambda/C_2$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Problem)

> **Problem:** An optical pyrometer uses a tungsten filament ($\epsilon_f = 0.42$) and is sighted on a steel target ($\epsilon = 0.35$) at wavelength $\lambda = 0.65\ \mu m$. At the null point, the filament temperature is $T_f = 1800\ K$. Find the true temperature of the steel.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- $\lambda = 0.65 \times 10^{-6}\ m$
- $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\ m\cdot K$
- $\epsilon = 0.35$ (steel)
- $T_f = 1800\ K$

**Applying the derived equation:**

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_f} + \frac{\lambda}{C_2} \cdot \ln(\epsilon)$$

**Step 1:** Compute $\frac{1}{T_f} = \frac{1}{1800} = 5.556 \times 10^{-4}\ K^{-1}$

**Step 2:** Compute $\frac{\lambda}{C_2}$:

$$\frac{\lambda}{C_2} = \frac{0.65 \times 10^{-6}}{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.518 \times 10^{-5}\ K^{-1}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $\ln(0.35) = -1.0498$

**Step 4:** Compute the correction term:

$$\frac{\lambda}{C_2} \cdot \ln(\epsilon) = (4.518 \times 10^{-5})(-1.0498) = -4.743 \times 10^{-5}\ K^{-1}$$

**Step 5:** Sum the two terms:

$$\frac{1}{T} = 5.556 \times 10^{-4} - 4.743 \times 10^{-5} = 5.082 \times 10^{-4}\ K^{-1}$$

**Step 6:** Invert to find $T$:

$$T = \frac{1}{5.082 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1967.7\ K$$

**Step 7:** Convert to Celsius:

$$T = 1967.7 - 273.15 = 1694.5°C$$

> [!TIP] **Verification:** True temperature ($1694.5°C$) is *higher* than the filament temperature ($1800 - 273 = 1527°C$) — confirming that for $\epsilon < 1$, the true temperature is *higher* than the brightness temperature. **Physically consistent.** ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Disappearing Filament Optical Pyrometer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[TARGET HOT BODY\nFurnace / Molten Metal] --> B[OBJECTIVE LENS\nF=80-100mm\nAperture Control]
    B --> C[RED FILTER\nLambda=0.65 micrometer\nMonochromatic]
    C --> D[REFERENCE FILAMENT\nTungsten Wire\nCalibrated Current]
    D --> E[EYEPIECE\nMagnification 10x-20x\nAperture Stop]
    E --> F[OBSERVERS EYE\nNull Detection\nBrightness Match]
    
    G[RHEOSTAT\nVariable Current\n0 to 5 Amp] --> D
    D --> H[CALIBRATED AMMETER\nOR\nTEMPERATURE DIAL\nDirect T reading]
    
    H --> I[TEMPERATURE OUTPUT\nDegree Celsius or Kelvin\n600 to 6000 Degree C]
    
    J[STANDARD BLACKBODY\nCalibration Source\nKnown Temperature] --> A
    
    style A fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#006064,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:1px
    style G fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#BF360C,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#F5F5F5,stroke:#424242,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading the Diagram**
> The hot target emits red radiation → objective lens focuses it → red filter isolates the $0.65\ \mu m$ wavelength → this is mixed optically with the reference tungsten filament → observer views through eyepiece and adjusts the rheostat until the filament "disappears" → the calibrated dial at that rheostat position gives the temperature directly.

---

### 4.2 Process Flow of Experimental Procedure (KTU Lab Viva Favourite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[STEP 1: Setup\nMount optical pyrometer\non tripod stand] --> P2[STEP 2: Focus Objective Lens\nSight on hot target\nImage should be sharp]
    P2 --> P3[STEP 3: Insert Red Filter\nSelects Lambda=0.65 micrometer]
    P3 --> P4[STEP 4: View Through Eyepiece\nSee target with filament superimposed]
    P4 --> P5{STEP 5: Filament Brighter or\nDimmer than Target?}
    P5 -->|Filament Brighter| P6[Reduce Rheostat Current\nSlowly]
    P5 -->|Filament Dimmer| P7[Increase Rheostat Current\nSlowly]
    P6 --> P8[STEP 6: Adjust until Filament\nDisappears into Target Background]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9[STEP 7: Read Temperature\nfrom Calibrated Dial]
    P9 --> P10[STEP 8: Remove Pyrometer\nSwitch off target source]
    P10 --> P11[STEP 9: Tabulate Observations\nMean of 3 readings]
    P11 --> P12[STEP 10: Plot Calibration Graph\nIf Required]
    
    style P1 fill:#C8E6C9
    style P5 fill:#FFF59D
    style P8 fill:#FFCCBC
    style P9 fill:#B3E5FC
    style P12 fill:#E1BEE7
```

---

### 4.3 Component-Level Schematic — Sectional View

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph O1[OPTICAL ASSEMBLY]
        L1[Objective Lens\nCollects Target Radiation] 
        FILT[Monochromatic Red Filter\nWavelength Selector]
        FIL[Reference Tungsten Filament\nLamp with Calibration Coil]
        L2[Eyepiece Lens\nMagnified Viewing]
    end
    
    subgraph E1[ELECTRICAL ASSEMBLY]
        BATT[Battery\n4V or 6V DC Source]

        AMM[Calibrated Ammeter\nOR Direct Temperature Dial]
        SW[On Off Toggle Switch]
    end
    
    subgraph OBS[OBSERVATION]
        OBSERVER[Observer Eye\nDetects Null Point]
    end
    
    BATT --> SW
    SW --> RHEO
    RHEO --> FIL
    FIL --> AMM
    AMM --> BATT
    
    FILT -. monochromatic light .-> OBSERVER

    FILT --> FIL
    FIL --> L2
    L2 --> OBSERVER
    
    style BATT fill:#FFCDD2

    style AMM fill:#B3E5FC

```

> [!TIP] **KTU Lab Viva Question Map**
> "What is the function of the red filter?" → *"It selects a narrow band of red wavelength ($\lambda \approx 0.65\ \mu m$) so that the comparison is monochromatic, making Planck's equation analytically tractable."*

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question]`
**Explain the working principle of a Disappearing Filament Optical Pyrometer with a neat block diagram. Mention its range and the physical principle on which it is based.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Mark-Wise Distribution):**

- **1 Mark — Physical Principle:**
  The instrument is based on the principle of **monochromatic brightness comparison** between the unknown hot target and a calibrated reference tungsten filament. At the null point, the filament's brightness exactly matches the target's brightness at wavelength $\lambda \approx 0.65\ \mu m$ (red region), making the filament "disappear" into the target image.

- **1 Mark — Construction & Working:**
  The instrument consists of an objective lens, an eyepiece, a red filter, a calibrated tungsten filament lamp, and a variable rheostat controlling the filament current. The target image is focused on the filament through the red filter. The observer adjusts the rheostat until the filament disappears. The temperature is read directly from the calibrated dial.

- **1 Mark — Range & Application:**
  The instrument measures temperatures in the range **$600°C$ to $6000°C$** without physical contact. The governing physical law is **Wien's Approximation** of Planck's Radiation Law.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Model Question]`
**State the role of the red filter in an optical pyrometer. Why is red light (and not blue or green) used for the comparison?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Mark-Wise Distribution):**

- **1 Mark — Function of Red Filter:**
  The red filter restricts the comparison to a narrow band of monochromatic radiation centered at $\lambda \approx 0.65\ \mu m$. This makes the brightness comparison wavelength-specific, allowing the use of **Wien's Approximation** (valid only for monochromatic radiation) for temperature calculation.

- **1 Mark — Why Red?**
  The red region of the visible spectrum offers the best compromise between **high pyrometric sensitivity** (sensitivity $\propto 1/\lambda$) and the **sensitivity of the human eye** (which peaks near 0.55 $\mu m$ and remains responsive at 0.65 $\mu m$).

- **1 Mark — Why Not Shorter Wavelengths?**
  Blue and green wavelengths (e.g., 0.45 $\mu m$) would require very high temperatures to produce sufficient visible brightness and would lie at the edge of human eye sensitivity. Longer wavelengths (infrared) are invisible to the eye, defeating the purpose of an *optical* (eye-based) instrument.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, With Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Style]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **disappearing filament optical pyrometer**. Discuss the role of the red filter, the rheostat, and the eyepiece in the measurement process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A disappearing filament optical pyrometer is sighted on a molten metal target. The pyrometer's red filter corresponds to $\lambda = 0.65\ \mu m$. The emissivity of the molten metal is $\epsilon = 0.40$. At the null point, the tungsten filament temperature is $T_f = 1900\ K$. Calculate:
   (i) The brightness temperature of the target.
   (ii) The true temperature of the target.
   (iii) The percentage error introduced if the emissivity correction is neglected. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working (7 Marks):**

**Construction (3.5 Marks):**
The disappearing filament optical pyrometer consists of:
- **Objective Lens:** A converging lens of focal length ~80-100 mm focuses the radiation from the hot target onto the plane of the reference filament.
- **Reference Filament:** A calibrated tungsten wire filament mounted in the focal plane of the objective lens, connected to a DC source through a variable rheostat and a calibrated ammeter.
- **Red Filter:** Placed in the path of the light beam to restrict the radiation to a narrow band centered at $\lambda \approx 0.65\ \mu m$.
- **Eyepiece:** A magnifying lens for observing the filament and target image clearly.
- **Rheostat:** A variable resistor used to adjust the filament current, which controls the filament's brightness.
- **Calibrated Scale:** A direct-reading temperature dial coupled to the rheostat.

**Working (3.5 Marks):**
The hot target is sighted through the objective lens, and its image is formed in the plane of the tungsten filament. The red filter ensures monochromatic comparison. The observer views both the target image and the filament simultaneously through the eyepiece. The rheostat is varied, which changes the filament current and hence its temperature and brightness:

- If **filament is too bright**, it appears as a bright line against the target.
- If **filament is too dim**, it appears as a dark line.
- At the **null point (filament disappears)**, the filament's brightness exactly matches the target's monochromatic brightness, and the calibrated dial gives the target temperature directly.

The relationship between filament current and temperature is pre-established by calibrating against a standard blackbody furnace at known temperatures.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given Data:**
- $\lambda = 0.65 \times 10^{-6}\ m$
- $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\ m\cdot K$
- $\epsilon = 0.40$ (molten metal)
- $T_f = 1900\ K$

**(i) Brightness Temperature (2 Marks):**

At null point, for a blackbody, the brightness temperature equals the filament temperature:

$$T_B = T_f = 1900\ K = 1626.85°C$$

**(ii) True Temperature (3 Marks):**

Using the corrected pyrometric equation:

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_f} + \frac{\lambda}{C_2} \cdot \ln(\epsilon)$$

Compute $\frac{1}{T_f} = \frac{1}{1900} = 5.2632 \times 10^{-4}\ K^{-1}$

Compute $\frac{\lambda}{C_2} = \frac{0.65 \times 10^{-6}}{1.4388 \times 10^{-2}} = 4.518 \times 10^{-5}\ K^{-1}$

Compute $\ln(0.40) = -0.9163$

Compute correction term:

$$\frac{\lambda}{C_2} \cdot \ln(\epsilon) = (4.518 \times 10^{-5})(-0.9163) = -4.140 \times 10^{-5}\ K^{-1}$$

Sum:

$$\frac{1}{T} = 5.2632 \times 10^{-4} - 4.140 \times 10^{-5} = 4.849 \times 10^{-4}\ K^{-1}$$

Therefore:

$$T = \frac{1}{4.849 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2062.3\ K = 1789.2°C$$

**(iii) Percentage Error (2 Marks):**

If emissivity is neglected, the indicated temperature is $T_f = 1900\ K$, but the true temperature is $T = 2062.3\ K$.

$$\text{Percentage Error} = \frac{T - T_f}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{2062.3 - 1900}{2062.3} \times 100\%$$

$$= \frac{162.3}{2062.3} \times 100\% \approx 7.87\%$$

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> **Common Error 1:** Students forget to convert $T_f$ to Kelvin before substitution. Always use $K$ in the pyrometric equation. **Common Error 2:** Students forget that $\ln(\epsilon)$ is *negative* (since $\epsilon < 1$), and they incorrectly add the term instead of subtracting. **Common Error 3:** In the percentage error formula, students use $(T - T_f)/T_f$ instead of the correct relative-to-true-value form. **Marks Lost Per Error: 1-2 marks each.**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style]`

**(a)** Derive the working equation of an optical pyrometer starting from **Planck's Radiation Law**, clearly stating the assumptions made. Show that for a blackbody target, the true temperature equals the brightness temperature. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **advantages, limitations, and sources of error** in the optical pyrometer method of temperature measurement. Compare it briefly with a **radiation pyrometer** and a **thermocouple** in terms of range, accuracy, and application. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** State Planck's Law:

$$E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** State the assumption: For $\lambda T$ small (e.g., $\lambda = 0.65\ \mu m$, $T > 1000\ K$), $e^{C_2/\lambda T} \gg 1$, so $e^{C_2/\lambda T} - 1 \approx e^{C_2/\lambda T}$. This gives **Wien's Approximation:**

$$E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) \approx \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** For the pyrometer, the brightness of the reference filament at temperature $T_f$:

$$E_{\lambda}^{filament} = \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T_f}$$

(The filament is approximated as a blackbody.)

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** For the target at temperature $T$ with emissivity $\epsilon$:

$$E_{\lambda}^{target} = \epsilon \cdot \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** At the null point (filament disappears):

$$\frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T_f} = \epsilon \cdot \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Cancel $C_1/\lambda^5$, take natural log, and rearrange:

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_f} + \frac{\lambda}{C_2} \cdot \ln(\epsilon)$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark):** For a **blackbody** ($\epsilon = 1$), $\ln(1) = 0$, hence:

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_f} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad T = T_f$$

i.e., the brightness temperature equals the true temperature. **QED.**

---

**(b) Discussion (7 Marks):**

**Advantages of Optical Pyrometer (2 Marks):**
1. **Non-contact measurement** — ideal for very hot, moving, or chemically reactive targets.
2. **Wide range** — measures from 600°C to 6000°C.
3. **Fast response** — measurement is instantaneous (sub-second).
4. **No interference with target** — does not disturb the measured process.
5. **Suitable for hazardous environments** — can be sighted from a safe distance.

**Limitations of Optical Pyrometer (1.5 Marks):**
1. Cannot measure temperatures below ~600°C (insufficient visible glow).
2. Reads **brightness temperature**, not true temperature (emissivity correction required).
3. Human eye is the detector — introduces subjective errors.
4. Smoke, dust, or vapor in the path can cause errors.
5. Requires the target to be visible (line of sight).

**Sources of Error (1 Mark):**
- Emissivity correction uncertainty (largest source).
- Object distance variation affecting apparent brightness.
- Atmospheric absorption and scattering between target and pyrometer.
- Filament aging causing calibration drift.
- Observer fatigue and color perception variation.

**Comparison Table (2.5 Marks):**

| Parameter | Optical Pyrometer | Radiation Pyrometer (Total) | Thermocouple |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------------|--------------|
| Sensing Principle | Monochromatic brightness | Total radiation (all $\lambda$) | Seebeck EMF |
| Range | 600°C – 6000°C | 500°C – 3000°C | -200°C – 2300°C |
| Accuracy | ±5°C – ±20°C | ±5°C – ±10°C | ±0.5°C – ±5°C |
| Contact Required | No | No | Yes (invasive) |
| Response Time | < 1 s | < 1 s | 1 s – 10 s |
| Emissivity Error | Yes (significant) | Yes (most significant) | None |
| Best Application | Steel, furnace, kiln | Boiler, flame temp | Process liquids, gases |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B**
> **Common Error 1:** Students write Planck's law but forget to state the **assumption** that justifies Wien's approximation. Examiner allocates 1 mark *specifically* for the assumption. **Common Error 2:** In the comparison table, students confuse "radiation pyrometer" with "optical pyrometer" — these are *different* instruments! Radiation pyrometer measures **total** radiation (using Stefan-Boltzmann law), while optical pyrometer measures **monochromatic** radiation. **Common Error 3:** Some students write the comparison as an essay instead of a structured table — examiner often deducts 0.5-1 mark for poor presentation.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT] **Memorize These Before the Exam:**

- [x] **Optical Pyrometer** = Non-contact, monochromatic brightness comparison, red light ($\lambda \approx 0.65\ \mu m$).
- [x] **Range:** 600°C to 6000°C. **Accuracy:** ±5°C to ±20°C.
- [x] **Working Principle:** Filament brightness is matched with target brightness; at null point, filament "disappears."
- [x] **Governing Equation (Wien's Approximation):** $E_{\lambda} \approx \frac{C_1}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot e^{-C_2/\lambda T}$
- [x] **Brightness Comparison Equation:** $\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_f} + \frac{\lambda}{C_2} \cdot \ln(\epsilon)$
- [x] **For blackbody:** $T = T_f$ (true temperature = brightness temperature).
- [x] **For non-blackbody:** $T > T_f$ (true temperature > brightness temperature).
- [x] **Second Radiation Constant:** $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\ m\cdot K$ — *memorize this number.*
- [x] **Why Red Light?** Highest pyrometric sensitivity in the visible range + human eye responsive.
- [x] **Role of Red Filter:** Makes comparison monochromatic — enables use of Wien's approximation.
- [x] **Calibration Source:** Standard blackbody furnace at known temperatures.
- [x] **Differences vs Radiation Pyrometer:** Optical = monochromatic (Wien's), Radiation = total (Stefan-Boltzmann).
- [x] **Differences vs Thermocouple:** Pyrometer = non-contact + high range; Thermocouple = contact + lower range but higher accuracy.
- [x] **Major Error Source:** Emissivity correction uncertainty.
- [x] **Cannot Measure Below 600°C** — always remember this range limitation in numerical/objective questions.

> [!TIP] **Last-Minute Exam Strategy**
> 1. Always convert temperatures to **Kelvin** before substitution in the pyrometric equation.
> 2. Always remember $\ln(\epsilon)$ is **negative** for $\epsilon < 1$ — leads to a *negative* correction term and a *higher* true temperature.
> 3. For "disappears" condition, state **Wien's approximation** explicitly — examiner rewards the physics.
> 4. In numericals, write down $C_2 = 1.4388 \times 10^{-2}\ m\cdot K$ first, before substituting — this shows command over the constant.
> 5. Always end the answer with a practical application sentence (e.g., "This principle is used in steel plants to measure molten steel temperature.") — adds 0.5-1 mark on KTU valuation.

**End of KTU Premium Notes — Module Experiment 2: Measurement of Temperature Using Optical Pyrometer**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of viscosity using Redwood viscometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL508 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION & IOT LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of viscosity using Redwood viscometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: MEASUREMENT OF VISCOSITY USING REDWOOD VISCOMETER

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Redwood Viscometer (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> The Redwood viscometer is a **standard efflux-type capillary viscometer** widely used in the petroleum, lubricant, and process industries to determine the **kinematic viscosity** of industrial oils and fluids by measuring the **efflux time** of a fixed volume (50 cm³) of the test liquid flowing through a precisely machined standard orifice under isothermal conditions.

The instrument is named after Sir Boverton Redwood, who standardized the apparatus. The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus classifies it as a **primary/secondary industrial instrument** under the domain of *Analytical & Quality Control Instrumentation* and is officially listed in the **BIPM (Bureau of Indian Standards IS: 1448 – P: 25)** classification for petroleum testing.

| Viscometer Class | Standard Reference | Efflux Volume | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Redwood No. 1** | IS 1448 P:25 | **50 cm³** | Light & medium oils |
| **Redwood No. 2** | IS 1448 P:25 | **50 cm³** | Heavy fuel oils, bitumen |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw Test"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of viscosity as the **"thickness resistance"** of a liquid. Imagine drinking water through a thin straw versus drinking cold honey through the *same* straw — honey is clearly more viscous because it resists flowing out of the straw.
>
> The **Redwood viscometer** is essentially a **controlled "straw" experiment**:
> - The **liquid** is your "honey or water"
> - The **standard orifice (1.62 mm)** acts as the "straw"
> - The **efflux time** for a fixed **50 cm³** volume is the measurable "resistance to flow"
> - The longer the time → the higher the viscosity
>
> The instrument converts this "time" into a precise physical quantity called **kinematic viscosity (ν) in centistokes (cSt) or mm²/s**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Test Parameters

- **Standard Test Temperature for India:** **60 °C (often 100 °F)** for fuel oils and lubricants.
- **Orifice diameter (No. 1):** **1.62 mm**
- **Orifice diameter (No. 2):** **10.0 mm**
- **Collection volume:** **50 cm³** (fixed Kohlrausch flask)
- **Test gravity constant:** **981 cm/s²** (used in derivations)
- **Universal constant (SI):** *g* = **9.81 m/s²**
- **Reference standard:** **IS 1448 (Part 25) / IP 70 / ASTM D 88**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Quick-Fact:** In board examinations, examiners expect students to remember that **Redwood viscometer gives kinematic viscosity (ν) directly**, and **dynamic viscosity (μ) is then computed as μ = ρ × ν**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Redwood viscometer operates on the **Hagen-Poiseuille principle of efflux flow combined with kinetic energy correction**. The liquid is allowed to discharge from a **cylindrical oil cup** through a **precision orifice** into a calibrated **Kohlrausch flask** of 50 cm³ capacity. The time taken is correlated to kinematic viscosity using an empirical formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hagen-Poiseuille's Law (the foundation):**
> $$\nu = \frac{\pi \cdot g \cdot r^{4} \cdot h \cdot t}{8 \cdot L \cdot V} - \frac{m \cdot V}{8 \cdot \pi \cdot L \cdot t}$$
>
> where the **first term** represents the **viscous (Poiseuille) flow contribution** and the **second term** represents the **kinetic energy (Hagenbach) correction** for the velocity of efflux.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Sequence

1. **Sample Loading:** The oil cup is filled with the test liquid up to the levelling peg (working volume = 200 cm³).
2. **Thermostatic Stabilization:** The bath is heated/stirred until the thermometer reads the **desired test temperature (T)** ± 0.2 °C, usually **60 °C** for industrial lubricants.
3. **Orifice Blockage Check:** The orifice is closed with a ball-valve or stopper to prevent premature flow.
4. **Stirring:** The bath stirrer ensures uniform temperature distribution throughout the bath fluid (water or ethylene glycol).
5. **Efflux Initiation:** The valve is opened, and a **stop-watch** is started **simultaneously**.
6. **Volume Collection:** Liquid is collected in the **Kohlrausch flask (50 cm³ ± 0.05 cm³)**.
7. **Time Recording:** As the **50 cm³ mark** is reached, the stop-watch is stopped — this time *t* is the **Redwood seconds**.
8. **Repetition:** The procedure is repeated **3 times** and the **mean efflux time** is computed.
9. **Viscosity Calculation:** The mean time *t* is substituted in the **empirical Redwood formula** to obtain kinematic viscosity.
10. **Density Determination:** The oil density (ρ) at the test temperature is found using a **hydrometer** or **pyknometer**, and dynamic viscosity is then computed.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize Before Exam!**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Kinematic Viscosity (CGS)** | $\nu = 0.0026 \, t - \dfrac{1.72}{t}$ | **Stokes (cm²/s)** | For Redwood No. 1; valid for 30 s ≤ *t* ≤ 2000 s |
| 2 | **Kinematic Viscosity (SI)** | $\nu = 0.26 \, t - \dfrac{172}{t}$ | **mm²/s (= cSt)** | Equivalent of #1 in SI |
| 3 | **Dynamic Viscosity** | $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$ | **Poise (g/cm·s)** or **Pa·s** | ρ in g/cm³ if ν in stokes |
| 4 | **SI Dynamic Viscosity** | $\mu \,[\text{Pa·s}] = \rho \,[\text{kg/m}^3] \times \nu \,[\text{m}^2/\text{s}]$ | **Pa·s** | Multiply stokes by 0.0001 to get m²/s |
| 5 | **Mean Efflux Time** | $\bar{t} = \dfrac{t_1 + t_2 + t_3}{3}$ | seconds | Always average ≥ 3 readings |
| 6 | **Standard Test Temp (India)** | $T = 60 \,°\text{C}$ (commonly) | °C | Other temps: 80 °C, 100 °C |
| 7 | **Hagen-Poiseuille Raw** | $\nu = \dfrac{\pi g r^4 h t}{8 L V} - \dfrac{m V}{8 \pi L t}$ | cm²/s | Theoretical basis |
| 8 | **Conversion: Stokes → cSt** | $1 \, \text{St} = 100 \, \text{cSt}$ | cSt | $1 \,\text{cSt} = 1 \,\text{mm}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 9 | **Conversion: Poise → Pa·s** | $1 \, \text{P} = 0.1 \, \text{Pa·s}$ | Pa·s | $1 \,\text{cP} = 1 \,\text{mPa·s}$ |
| 10 | **Apparatus Orifice** | *d* = 1.62 mm (No. 1); 10 mm (No. 2) | mm | Fixed by IS 1448 |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Refineries:** Quality control of lubricating oils, fuel oils, transformer oils.
- **Power Plants:** Monitoring **turbine oil** and **governor oil** for thermal power stations.
- **Paint & Varnish Industry:** Consistency of coatings and inks.
- **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing:** Quality grading of glycerol, syrups, and base fluids.
- **Polymer Processing:** Monitoring melt-flow behavior in extrusion plants.
- **Dairy & Food Industry:** Thickness grading of cream, honey, sauces.
- **Automotive Industry:** Engine oil grading (SAE 20W-40 etc. derived via viscometer data).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Empirical Redwood Formula

We begin from **Hagen-Poiseuille's equation** for laminar flow through a capillary (orifice), and apply the **Hagenbach kinetic-energy correction**, then substitute standard Redwood No. 1 geometric constants to obtain the empirical formula.

#### STEP 1: Starting Equation (Purely Viscous, No Kinetic Correction)

For a liquid draining under a constant head *h* through a tube of length *L* and radius *r*:

$$
\nu = \frac{\pi \cdot g \cdot r^{4} \cdot h \cdot t}{8 \cdot L \cdot V}
$$

> *Line-by-line interpretation:*
> - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity (driving force)
> - $r^4$ = fourth-power dependence on orifice radius (geometry)
> - $h$ = effective hydraulic head
> - $t$ = efflux time
> - $V$ = collected volume (50 cm³ for Redwood)
> - $L$ = effective capillary length

#### STEP 2: Inclusion of Kinetic Energy Correction

Because the liquid leaves the orifice with finite velocity, a fraction of the head is "lost" as kinetic energy, reducing the effective pressure. The **Hagenbach correction** term is:

$$
\nu = \frac{\pi \cdot g \cdot r^{4} \cdot h \cdot t}{8 \cdot L \cdot V} - \frac{m \cdot V}{8 \cdot \pi \cdot L \cdot t}
$$

where *m* is a coefficient related to the velocity profile (≈ 1.12 for Redwood No. 1).

#### STEP 3: Substitution of Standard Geometric Constants

For the **Redwood No. 1 viscometer**, the fixed dimensions and coefficients are:

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Orifice radius (*r*) | 0.081 (from *d* = 1.62 mm) | cm |
| Orifice length (*L*) | 0.315 | cm |
| Mean head (*h*) | 7.0 | cm |
| Kinetic coefficient (*m*) | 1.12 | — |
| Collection volume (*V*) | 50 | cm³ |

#### STEP 4: Simplification to the Empirical Form

After substituting these into the full equation, all the geometric terms collapse into two **empirical constants** (A and B), giving the working formula:

$$
\nu = A \cdot t - \frac{B}{t}
$$

> *Conversion logic:*
> - The first term *A·t* = "viscous time" contribution
> - The second term *B/t* = "kinetic energy" loss correction
> - A and B are dimensionless/lumped constants determined experimentally for each viscometer

#### STEP 5: Final Empirical Constants for Redwood No. 1

Substituting the values for Redwood No. 1 yields:

$$
A = 0.0026 \quad \text{and} \quad B = 1.72
$$

Therefore, the **final working equation** is:

$$
\boxed{\;\nu = 0.0026 \, t \; - \; \frac{1.72}{t}\;}
$$

where the result is in **Stokes (cm²/s)** and *t* is in **seconds (Redwood seconds)**.

#### STEP 6: Dynamic Viscosity (μ) Derivation

Dynamic viscosity is the **product of density and kinematic viscosity**:

$$
\mu = \rho \cdot \nu
$$

> *Why this works:* Kinematic viscosity measures the resistance to flow under gravity alone (inertia-dominated), while dynamic viscosity measures the **internal shear stress** per unit rate of strain. The relation μ = ρ·ν converts one to the other via the fluid's mass density.

For Redwood's standard CGS form:

$$
\mu \,[\text{poise}] = \rho \,[\text{g/cm}^3] \times \nu \,[\text{stokes}]
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (Step-by-Step)

> [!TIP]
> **Sample Problem (model exam pattern):**
> A lubricating oil has an efflux time of **75 seconds** through Redwood No. 1 viscometer at **60 °C**. The density of the oil at 60 °C is **0.86 g/cm³**. Calculate the **kinematic** and **dynamic** viscosity.

#### Step A: Kinematic Viscosity Calculation

$$
\nu = 0.0026 \, t - \frac{1.72}{t}
$$

$$
\nu = 0.0026 \times 75 - \frac{1.72}{75}
$$

$$
\nu = 0.1950 - 0.02293
$$

$$
\nu = 0.17207 \, \text{Stokes}
$$

> *Conversion logic:* First compute 0.0026 × 75 = 0.195; then 1.72 ÷ 75 = 0.02293; subtract to get 0.17207 St.

In centistokes (cSt):

$$
\nu = 0.17207 \times 100 = 17.207 \, \text{cSt} \;\; (\text{or } \, \text{mm}^2/\text{s})
$$

#### Step B: Dynamic Viscosity Calculation

$$
\mu = \rho \cdot \nu
$$

$$
\mu = 0.86 \times 0.17207
$$

$$
\mu = 0.14798 \, \text{poise}
$$

In **centipoise (cP):**

$$
\mu = 0.14798 \times 100 = 14.798 \, \text{cP}
$$

In **SI units (Pa·s):**

$$
\mu = 0.14798 \times 0.1 = 0.014798 \, \text{Pa·s}
$$

#### Final Answer Summary

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Efflux time (t)** | 75 s |
| **Kinematic viscosity (ν)** | **0.172 St** = 17.21 cSt |
| **Density (ρ)** | 0.86 g/cm³ |
| **Dynamic viscosity (μ)** | **0.148 poise** = 14.8 cP = 0.0148 Pa·s |

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Redwood vs. Other Viscometers

| Feature | **Redwood** | **Saybolt** | **Engler** | **Ubbelohde** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Origin** | UK (B. Redwood) | USA (G. M. Saybolt) | Germany (C. Engler) | Germany (L. Ubbelohde) |
| **Standard Temp** | 60 °C | 100 °F / 130 °F | 20 °C, 50 °C, 100 °C | 40 °C |
| **Collection Vol.** | 50 cm³ | 60 cm³ | 200 cm³ | 10–15 cm³ (capillary) |
| **Orifice / Capillary** | 1.62 mm | 1.765 mm | 2.9 mm | ~0.5 mm glass |
| **Output** | **Redwood seconds** | Saybolt seconds | Engler degrees | Direct cSt |
| **Best for** | Petroleum oils | Petroleum (USA) | Heavy oils, bitumen | Pure liquids, polymers |
| **BIS/IS Standard** | IS 1448 P:25 | ASTM D 88 | DIN 51560 | ASTM D 445 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Redwood Viscometer

> [!NOTE]
> The figure below illustrates the **inter-component functional flow** of a Redwood viscometer assembly, including the thermostatic bath, oil cup, orifice, collection flask, and instrumentation chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Power Supply 230V AC] --> B[Heater Element immersed in bath]
    B --> C[Thermostatic Bath water or oil]
    C --> D[Stirrer Motor with impeller]
    D --> C
    E[Thermometer Pt100 or Mercury] --> F[Temperature Indicator Controller]
    F --> B
    G[Oil Cup sample volume 200 cm3] --> H[Agate Orifice diameter 1.62 mm]
    H --> I[Stop Watch Start]
    I --> J[Kohlrausch Flask capacity 50 cm3]
    J --> K[Stop Watch Stop at 50 cm3 mark]
    K --> L[Mean Efflux Time t seconds]
    L --> M[Apply Empirical Formula nu equals 0.0026 t minus 1.72 over t]
    M --> N[Kinematic Viscosity nu in Stokes]
    G --> O[Hydrometer or Pyknometer]
    O --> P[Sample Density rho at test temperature]
    P --> Q[Compute Dynamic Viscosity mu equals rho times nu]
    N --> Q
    Q --> R[Final Report Redwood seconds cSt and cP]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **left vertical chain** (A → C → B → F) represents the **thermostatic control loop** ensuring isothermal test conditions.
- The **central chain** (G → H → J → L) is the **efflux flow path** — the actual measurement channel.
- The **lower chain** (O → P → Q) is the **density-to-dynamic-viscosity conversion** branch.
- All branches converge at **R**, the final reported values in three units (seconds, cSt, cP).

### 4.2 Schematic Cross-Section of the Oil Cup Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Oil Cup cylindrical brass] --> B[Level Peg standard fill mark]
    A --> C[Cover Lid with thermometer slot]
    A --> D[Outlet Orifice 1.62 mm Redwood No 1]
    D --> E[Ball Valve discharge control]
    E --> F[Efflux Jet into receiving flask]
    G[Water Jacket surrounding oil cup] --> A
    H[Bath Stirrer] --> G
    I[Bath Heater] --> G
    J[Levelling Screws base of instrument] --> A
```

### 4.3 Process-Level Data Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sampling from storage tank] --> B[Filtration to remove particulates]
    B --> C[Pour into oil cup up to peg]
    C --> D[Stabilize temperature for 10 minutes]
    D --> E{Is temperature within 0.2 degree of test T}
    E -- Yes --> F[Open orifice and start stopwatch]
    E -- No --> G[Adjust heater or wait]
    G --> D
    F --> H[Collect 50 cm3 in Kohlrausch flask]
    H --> I[Stop stopwatch and record t1]
    I --> J[Repeat two more times for t2 and t3]
    J --> K[Compute mean t equals t1 plus t2 plus t3 over 3]
    K --> L[Apply formula nu equals 0.0026 t minus 1.72 over t]
    L --> M[Compute mu equals rho times nu]
    M --> N[Generate Lab Report with CO mapping]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Lab Records:** The data-flow diagram above maps directly to the **CO3 (Apply standard viscometric methods for industrial fluids)** of the PCICL508 syllabus. Each block is a procedural step that should be written in your lab record under the heading *"Procedure with Flowchart"*.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Define kinematic viscosity. State the empirical formula used to calculate kinematic viscosity from Redwood viscometer readings.**

**Model Answer:**
Kinematic viscosity is defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of a fluid. It represents the fluid's resistance to flow under the action of gravity, expressed as $\nu = \mu / \rho$, with units of **Stokes (cm²/s)** or **centistokes (mm²/s)**.
The empirical formula for **Redwood No. 1 viscometer** is:
$$\nu = 0.0026 \, t - \frac{1.72}{t}$$
where *t* is the efflux time in seconds for 50 cm³ of liquid.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List any four components of a Redwood viscometer and state the standard test temperature used in Indian industrial practice.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Oil cup** (brass, working capacity 200 cm³)
2. **Agate orifice** (1.62 mm diameter, interchangeable with No. 2 of 10 mm)
3. **Kohlrausch flask** (50 cm³ ± 0.05 cm³, with calibration mark)
4. **Thermostatic water/oil bath** (with heater, stirrer, and thermometer)
5. **Levelling screws** and **stopper/ball valve**

**Standard test temperature (India):** **60 °C** (also commonly used: 80 °C, 100 °C, 120 °C).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Modified]

> An industrial lubricant is tested in a Redwood No. 1 viscometer at **60 °C** and the following efflux times are recorded for three trials:
>
> | Trial | 1 | 2 | 3 |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Time (s) | 68 | 70 | 72 |
>
> The density of the lubricant at 60 °C is found to be **0.88 g/cm³** using a pyknometer.

**(a)** Calculate the **mean efflux time** and the **kinematic viscosity** of the lubricant.
**(b)** Hence determine the **dynamic viscosity** in **centipoise** and **Pa·s**, and comment on the suitability of the oil for **high-speed bearing lubrication** at 60 °C.

#### Model Solution:

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Mean Efflux Time**
$$\bar{t} = \frac{t_1 + t_2 + t_3}{3} = \frac{68 + 70 + 72}{3} = \frac{210}{3} = 70 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 2: Apply Redwood Empirical Formula**
$$\nu = 0.0026 \, \bar{t} - \frac{1.72}{\bar{t}}$$
$$\nu = (0.0026 \times 70) - \left(\frac{1.72}{70}\right)$$
$$\nu = 0.1820 - 0.02457$$
$$\nu = 0.15743 \, \text{Stokes} \;(\text{cm}^2/\text{s})$$

**Step 3: Convert to Centistokes**
$$\nu = 0.15743 \times 100 = 15.743 \, \text{cSt} \approx 15.74 \, \text{mm}^2/\text{s}$$

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Dynamic Viscosity in CGS (poise)**
$$\mu = \rho \cdot \nu = 0.88 \times 0.15743 = 0.13854 \, \text{poise}$$

**Step 2: Convert to Centipoise**
$$\mu = 0.13854 \times 100 = 13.854 \, \text{cP} \approx 13.85 \, \text{cP}$$

**Step 3: Convert to SI (Pa·s)**
$$\mu = 0.13854 \times 0.1 = 0.013854 \, \text{Pa·s} \approx 0.0139 \, \text{Pa·s}$$

**Step 4: Engineering Suitability Comment**
A dynamic viscosity of ~13.85 cP at 60 °C indicates the oil is in the **light-to-medium industrial lubricant range (SAE 20 grade)**. For high-speed bearing lubrication, the **acceptable range is typically 10–30 cP at operating temperature**. Since 13.85 cP lies within this band, the oil is **suitable** for the application, provided it maintains an adequate **viscosity index (VI)** and oxidative stability.

---

> **Question B** [Alternative Choice – KTU 2024 Pattern]

> **(a)** With a **neat labeled block diagram**, describe the construction and working of a **Redwood viscometer No. 1**. State the formula used and the units of kinematic viscosity obtained. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A fuel oil sample shows an efflux time of **138 seconds** at 100 °C. Density of oil at 100 °C is **0.92 g/cm³**. Compute kinematic and dynamic viscosity, and discuss how the result would change if **Redwood No. 2** were used instead. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

##### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Construction:** The Redwood viscometer No. 1 consists of:
- A **brass oil cup** of 200 cm³ capacity with a **level peg** indicating the working volume.
- An **agate orifice** of diameter **1.62 mm** fitted at the bottom.
- A **ball valve / stopper** to control the start/stop of efflux.
- A **water bath (or oil bath for high temperatures)** with heater, stirrer, and thermometer for temperature control.
- A **Kohlrasch flask** of exactly 50 cm³ capacity to collect the efflux.
- **Levelling screws** at the base to ensure vertical alignment.

**Working:** The cup is filled with the test liquid to the level peg and brought to the desired test temperature (e.g., 60 °C). The orifice is opened, and a stopwatch is started. The time taken for **50 cm³** of liquid to collect in the Kohlrausch flask is the **Redwood seconds (t)**. The kinematic viscosity is computed from the empirical formula:
$$\nu = 0.0026 \, t - \frac{1.72}{t} \quad [\text{in Stokes} = \text{cm}^2/\text{s}]$$

##### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1: Kinematic Viscosity (No. 1 viscometer)**
$$\nu = 0.0026(138) - \frac{1.72}{138} = 0.3588 - 0.01246 = 0.3463 \, \text{Stokes} = 34.63 \, \text{cSt}$$

**Step 2: Dynamic Viscosity**
$$\mu = 0.92 \times 0.3463 = 0.3186 \, \text{poise} = 31.86 \, \text{cP} = 0.03186 \, \text{Pa·s}$$

**Step 3: Effect of Using Redwood No. 2**

| Feature | Redwood No. 1 | Redwood No. 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Orifice | 1.62 mm | 10.0 mm |
| Efflux time | Longer (138 s here) | **Much shorter** (typically 15–40 s) |
| Empirical constants | A = 0.0026, B = 1.72 | **Different (A = 0.027, B = 0.32 approx.)** |
| Best for | Light/medium oils | **Heavy fuel oils, residual oils** |

If No. 2 were used, the efflux time would **drop drastically (approx. 1/5th to 1/10th of No. 1)**, and the empirical constants would be re-derived. The kinematic viscosity (a **fluid property**) would **remain numerically identical** (assuming the temperature is the same), but the **measured Redwood seconds** would differ — illustrating that the orifice size changes the *efflux time* but not the actual fluid viscosity.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls!**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks):** Students often write ν in stokes but forget to convert to cSt or Pa·s. Always state units clearly. KTU expects **all three units** (St, cSt, Pa·s) in long answers.
> 2. **Formula Inversion (–3 marks):** Writing $\nu = 1.72/t - 0.0026t$ is a common error — the empirical formula is **strictly** $0.0026t - 1.72/t$, *not* the reverse.
> 3. **Skipping the kinetic correction term:** Examiners allocate **2 marks** specifically for explaining the Hagenbach correction in derivations — do not omit it.
> 4. **Confusing Redwood No. 1 and No. 2 constants:** A = 0.0026 and B = 1.72 are **only** for No. 1. Using them for No. 2 is a **3-mark deduction** disaster.
> 5. **Forgetting to average multiple trials:** A single time reading is **not acceptable** — always show $\bar{t} = (t_1 + t_2 + t_3)/3$.
> 6. **Density confusion:** Always pair ρ [g/cm³] with ν [stokes] → μ [poise]. Mixing units is the most common numerical error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🧠 RAPID-FIRE REVISION POINTS — Read 1 Hour Before Exam**

- ⭐ **Viscosity** is the internal resistance of a fluid to flow.
- ⭐ **Redwood viscometer** is an **efflux-type (NOT capillary-type)** viscometer.
- ⭐ The instrument directly measures **efflux time *t***, and viscosity is calculated **empirically**.
- ⭐ **Standard collection volume = 50 cm³** (Kohlrausch flask).
- ⭐ **Standard test temperature (India) = 60 °C.**
- ⭐ **Orifice diameter (No. 1) = 1.62 mm;** (No. 2) = **10 mm.**
- ⭐ **Empirical formula (No. 1):** $\nu = 0.0026t - 1.72/t$ — in **Stokes**.
- ⭐ **Dynamic viscosity:** $\mu = \rho \cdot \nu$ — in **poise** or **Pa·s**.
- ⭐ **1 St = 100 cSt = 100 mm²/s.**
- ⭐ **1 poise = 0.1 Pa·s = 100 cP.**
- ⭐ The formula is based on **Hagen-Poiseuille + Hagenbach kinetic correction.**
- ⭐ The formula is **valid for 30 s ≤ t ≤ 2000 s** (out of this range, apply corrections).
- ⭐ A **stirrer + heater + thermometer** maintain **isothermal conditions** — this is critical for accuracy.
- ⭐ **Kohlrausch flask** is the standardized 50 cm³ receiver — the experiment's volumetric reference.
- ⭐ **Apparatus standards:** **IS 1448 (Part 25) / IP 70 / ASTM D 88.**
- ⭐ **KTU 2024 Lab Outcome Mapping:** This experiment maps to **CO3 — Apply standard viscometric methods for industrial fluids.**
- ⭐ **Practical Tip:** Always rinse the cup with the sample liquid **before** the actual run to avoid dilution/contamination.
- ⭐ **Quick sanity check:** A typical lubricating oil gives ν between **10–100 cSt** at 60 °C; values outside this range indicate either experimental error or unusual fluid.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic to remember the formula:**
> **"0-0-2-6 times t, minus 1-7-2 over t"** → $0.0026t - 1.72/t$
> Just remember the two numbers: **26** and **172** — then place the decimal (0.) and divide accordingly.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of viscosity using rotational viscometer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL508 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION & IOT LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Measurement of viscosity using rotational viscometer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Viscosity

**Viscosity** is defined as the internal frictional resistance offered by a fluid to the relative motion of its adjacent layers. Mathematically, it is expressed by **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress developed in the fluid (N/m² or Pa)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic (absolute) viscosity of the fluid (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient perpendicular to the direction of flow (s⁻¹), also called the **shear rate** ($\dot{\gamma}$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** The ratio of shear stress to shear rate. SI unit: **Pa·s** (Pascal-second). CGS unit: **Poise (P)** where 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s. Commonly used sub-unit: **centipoise (cP)** where 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s.

> [!NOTE]
> **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** Defined as the ratio of dynamic viscosity to density: $\nu = \mu / \rho$. SI unit: **m²/s**. CGS unit: **Stokes (St)** where 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s.

## 1.2 Definition of a Rotational Viscometer

A **rotational viscometer** is an instrument used to measure the dynamic viscosity of a fluid by immersing a rotating element (called a **spindle**, **bob**, or **rotor**) into the fluid and measuring the **viscous drag torque** that the fluid exerts on it. The torque required to rotate the spindle at a constant angular velocity is directly proportional to the viscosity of the fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (PCICL508):** Students must be able to (a) state the principle of operation of a rotational viscometer, (b) identify the components of the experimental setup, (c) perform the experiment, record readings, and calculate viscosity using the **instrument factor table** or **theoretical formula**, and (d) appreciate the use of calibration standards.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Honey and the Spoon"

Imagine dipping a spoon into a jar of **honey** and stirring, and then doing the same with a jar of **water**:

- The **honey resists the motion of the spoon** much more strongly than water. You can feel a heavy "drag" on your hand. That drag is the **viscous torque**.
- The water barely resists — your hand moves freely.
- A rotational viscometer automates this exact "feel test" by replacing your hand with a **calibrated spring/torque sensor** and your eye with a **dial/digital readout**.

So, a rotational viscometer is essentially a **"machine spoon"** that quantifies the stickiness of a fluid by measuring the torque needed to rotate a paddle inside it at a known speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constant:** Dynamic viscosity of **water at 20 °C** = **1.002 cP** (often used as a calibration standard in labs).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle of Operation — Step-by-Step Logic

The measurement of viscosity using a rotational viscometer is based on the **torque balance** between the motor-driven spindle and the viscous resistance of the fluid:

1. **Immersion of the spindle:** A spindle of known geometry (cylindrical, disc, or T-shaped) is immersed in the test fluid contained in a beaker/cup up to a specified depth.
2. **Rotation at constant angular velocity:** A synchronous motor drives the spindle at a fixed angular velocity $\omega$ (rad/s) through a calibrated beryllium-copper spring.
3. **Viscous drag generation:** As the spindle rotates, the fluid adheres to its surface (no-slip condition). Layer-by-layer, shear is transmitted, and the fluid exerts a **resisting torque** $T$ on the spindle.
4. **Spring deflection:** The viscous torque twists the beryllium-copper spring by an angle $\theta$. A pointer attached to the spring deflects on a calibrated dial.
5. **Equilibrium condition:** The motor torque is balanced by the spring restoring torque, which is balanced by the viscous torque:
$$T_{motor} = T_{spring} = T_{viscous}$$
6. **Measurement:** The pointer deflection $\theta$ is read on the dial. The viscosity is computed as:
$$\mu = f(\theta, \omega, \text{spindle geometry})$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why this works:** For a **Newtonian fluid** (constant $\mu$), the viscous torque is linear in $\omega$ and in $\mu$. By keeping $\omega$ fixed, $\mu$ becomes directly proportional to the dial reading — making the instrument a **direct-reading device** when used with the manufacturer's factor table.

## 2.2 Working Equation — Concentric Cylinder Geometry

The most mathematically clean derivation assumes two concentric cylinders (the **bob** of radius $R_1$ and the **cup** of radius $R_2$). At angular velocity $\omega$, the velocity at the bob surface is $v = \omega R_1$, and the gap is $(R_2 - R_1)$. The velocity gradient in the gap is approximately:

$$\frac{du}{dy} \approx \frac{\omega R_1}{R_2 - R_1}$$

The shear stress on the bob surface is $\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{\omega R_1}{R_2 - R_1}$. The torque is force × arm, integrated over the bob surface (lateral area $2\pi R_1 L$):

$$T = \tau \cdot (2\pi R_1 L) \cdot R_1$$

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2\pi \mu L \omega R_1^3}{R_2 - R_1}}$$

> This is the **fundamental working equation** of a concentric-cylinder rotational viscometer.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Petroleum & Lubricants** | Quality control of engine oils, greases, fuels |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Consistency of syrups, ointments, injectables |
| **Food & Beverages** | Texture control of sauces, chocolate, dairy |
| **Paints & Coatings** | Flow, brush-ability, and leveling of paints |
| **Cosmetics** | Creams, lotions, shampoo quality |
| **Polymer Processing** | Molten polymer viscosity for extrusion control |
| **Cement/Construction** | Slump and workability of fresh concrete |
| **IoT Integration** | Modern viscometers output 4-20 mA / Modbus data for SCADA monitoring |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these for the KTU Lab Exam & University ESE.**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Newton's law of viscosity | $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ | Pa, Pa·s, s⁻¹ | Fundamental |
| 2 | Dynamic viscosity (SI) | $\mu$ | **Pa·s** (N·s/m²) | 1 Pa·s = 10 P = 1000 cP |
| 3 | Dynamic viscosity (CGS) | $\mu$ | **Poise (P)** | 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s |
| 4 | Kinematic viscosity | $\nu = \mu / \rho$ | m²/s or **Stokes (St)** | 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s |
| 5 | Viscous torque (concentric cyl.) | $T = \dfrac{2\pi \mu L \omega R_1^3}{R_2 - R_1}$ | N·m | $L$ = bob length, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 6 | Viscous torque (disc spindle) | $T = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi \mu \omega R^3$ | N·m | For a flat disc of radius $R$ in infinite fluid |
| 7 | Viscosity from dial reading | $\mu = (\text{Dial Reading}) \times (\text{Factor})$ | cP | **Most commonly used in lab** |
| 8 | Angular velocity | $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ | rad/s | $N$ = rpm |
| 9 | Conversion | $1 \text{ cP} = 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s} = 10^{-3} \text{ N·s/m}^2$ | — | Often appears in numericals |
| 10 | % Error | $\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{\vert \mu_{exp} - \mu_{std}\vert}{\mu_{std}} \times 100$ | — | Use `\vert` for absolute value notation |

## 2.5 Components of a Rotational Viscometer (Brookfield-type)

1. **Synchronous motor** – Provides constant rpm (e.g., 0.3, 0.6, 1.5, 3, 6, 12, 30, 60 rpm).
2. **Beryllium-copper spring** – Converts viscous torque into angular deflection.
3. **Spindle (rotor/bob)** – Interchangeable spindles LV-1 to LV-4 (for low-viscosity) or HA/HB (for high-viscosity).
4. **Pointer and graduated dial** – 0 to 100 divisions; reads torque deflection.
5. **Guard leg** – Prevents wall effects and ensures correct immersion depth.
6. **Sample cup** – Holds the test fluid (typically 600 mL beaker).
7. **Spirit level / levelling feet** – Ensures vertical spindle alignment.
8. **Digital readout (modern versions)** – Direct display in cP or mPa·s.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Viscous Torque on a Concentric Cylinder Bob

**Setup:** A bob of radius $R_1$ and length $L$ rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ inside a stationary cup of radius $R_2$, completely filled with a Newtonian fluid of viscosity $\mu$.

**Step 1 — Velocity at bob surface (no-slip condition):**
The tangential velocity of the fluid layer touching the bob is:
$$v = \omega R_1$$

**Step 2 — Approximate velocity gradient across the gap:**
Assuming a linear velocity profile across the small gap $(R_2 - R_1)$:
$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{\omega R_1}{R_2 - R_1}$$

**Step 3 — Shear stress on the bob (Newton's law):**
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \cdot \frac{\omega R_1}{R_2 - R_1}$$

**Step 4 — Differential force on a ring element:**
Consider a thin ring of the bob's lateral surface at radius $R_1$ and width $dz$. Its area is $dA = 2\pi R_1 \, dz$. The differential force on this ring is:
$$dF = \tau \cdot dA = \mu \cdot \frac{\omega R_1}{R_2 - R_1} \cdot 2\pi R_1 \, dz$$

**Step 5 — Differential torque on the ring (force × moment arm):**
$$dT = R_1 \cdot dF = \frac{2\pi \mu \omega R_1^3}{R_2 - R_1} \, dz$$

**Step 6 — Integrate over the bob length $L$:**
$$T = \int_{0}^{L} dT = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{2\pi \mu \omega R_1^3}{R_2 - R_1} \, dz$$

The integrand is independent of $z$ (assuming uniform immersion), so:

$$\boxed{T = \frac{2\pi \mu \omega L R_1^3}{R_2 - R_1}}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for viscosity:**
Rearranging for $\mu$:

$$\mu = \frac{T (R_2 - R_1)}{2\pi \omega L R_1^3}$$

> This is the theoretical form. In commercial Brookfield viscometers, the constants $(R_2-R_1)$, $L$, $R_1$ are absorbed into a single **multiplier (Factor)** supplied by the manufacturer for each spindle–speed combination, giving the practical form: **$\mu = \text{Dial Reading} \times \text{Factor}$**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion of rpm to rad/s

The dial is calibrated assuming a specific rotation rate. To compare with SI-based formulae:

$$\omega \, (\text{rad/s}) = \frac{2\pi N}{60}$$

where $N$ is the rotational speed in revolutions per minute (rpm).

**Example:** For $N = 60$ rpm:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 60}{60} = 2\pi \approx 6.283 \text{ rad/s}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1 (Direct Factor Method)

A Brookfield viscometer with **spindle LV-2 rotating at 30 rpm** gives a steady dial reading of **42 divisions**. The manufacturer's factor for this spindle–speed combination is **20 cP per division**. Calculate the dynamic viscosity of the fluid in **cP, Poise, and Pa·s**.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Use the factor method:
$$\mu \, (\text{cP}) = \text{Dial Reading} \times \text{Factor} = 42 \times 20 = 840 \text{ cP}$$

Step 2 — Convert to Poise (1 P = 100 cP):
$$\mu = \frac{840}{100} = 8.4 \text{ Poise} = 8.4 \text{ P}$$

Step 3 — Convert to Pa·s (1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s):
$$\mu = 840 \times 10^{-3} = 0.840 \text{ Pa·s}$$

**Answer:** $\mu = 840 \text{ cP} = 8.4 \text{ P} = 0.840 \text{ Pa·s}$.

---

### Example 2 (Theoretical Formula Method)

A rotational viscometer has a bob of radius $R_1 = 1.5$ cm and length $L = 5$ cm rotating inside a cup of radius $R_2 = 2.0$ cm. The rotational speed is $N = 60$ rpm. The measured torque is $T = 4.5 \times 10^{-3}$ N·m. Find the dynamic viscosity of the fluid in Pa·s and cP.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert rpm to rad/s:
$$\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 60}{60} = 2\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 6.283 \text{ rad/s}$$

Step 2 — Convert all lengths to SI units:
$R_1 = 0.015$ m, $R_2 = 0.020$ m, $L = 0.05$ m

Step 3 — Compute the gap:
$R_2 - R_1 = 0.020 - 0.015 = 0.005$ m

Step 4 — Substitute into the working equation and solve for $\mu$:
$$\mu = \frac{T (R_2 - R_1)}{2\pi \omega L R_1^3}$$

$$\mu = \frac{(4.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times (0.005)}{2\pi \times 6.283 \times 0.05 \times (0.015)^3}$$

Step 5 — Evaluate the denominator:
$2\pi \times 6.283 = 39.478$
$0.05 \times (0.015)^3 = 0.05 \times 3.375 \times 10^{-6} = 1.6875 \times 10^{-7}$
Denominator $= 39.478 \times 1.6875 \times 10^{-7} = 6.662 \times 10^{-6}$

Step 6 — Compute $\mu$:
$$\mu = \frac{(4.5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.005}{6.662 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{-5}}{6.662 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 3.378 \text{ Pa·s}$$

Step 7 — Convert to cP:
$$\mu = 3.378 \times 1000 = 3378 \text{ cP}$$

**Answer:** $\mu \approx 3.378 \text{ Pa·s} = 3378 \text{ cP}$.

---

### Example 3 (% Error with Calibration Standard)

A standard calibration oil of known viscosity $\mu_{std} = 500$ cP is tested on a rotational viscometer. The student measures 488 cP. Calculate the percentage error.

**Solution:**
$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert \mu_{exp} - \mu_{std}\vert}{\mu_{std}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{ Error} = \frac{\vert 488 - 500 \vert}{500} \times 100 = \frac{12}{500} \times 100 = 2.4\%$$

**Answer:** % Error = **2.4%** (acceptable for a lab experiment; KTU tolerance is typically ±5%).

---

## 3.4 Tabular Observation Format (KTU Lab Record Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Always include this table in your lab record for full marks.**

| Sl. No. | Fluid Sample | Spindle Used | Speed (rpm) | Dial Reading (div) | Multiplier (cP/div) | Viscosity (cP) | Mean Viscosity (cP) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sample A | LV-2 | 30 | 42 | 20 | 840 | 840 |
| 2 | Sample A | LV-2 | 30 | 41 | 20 | 820 | — |
| 3 | Sample A | LV-2 | 30 | 43 | 20 | 860 | — |
| 4 | Calibration oil (std) | LV-2 | 30 | 25 | 20 | 500 | 500 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a rotational viscometer** — from the power input through the fluid interaction to the viscosity output. This is the standard block-diagram representation expected in the KTU lab record.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Mains Supply 230V 50Hz] --> B[Regulated DC Power Supply]
    B --> C[Synchronous AC Motor]
    C --> D[Step Pulley and Belt Drive]
    D --> E[Calibrated Beryllium Copper Spring]
    E --> F[Spindle Rotor Immersed in Test Fluid]
    G[Test Fluid in Sample Cup] --> F
    F --> H[Viscous Drag Torque on Spindle]
    H --> E
    E --> I[Pointer Deflection on Graduated Dial]
    I --> J[Dial Reading Theta div]
    J --> K[Apply Manufacturer Factor or Theoretical Equation]
    K --> L[Dynamic Viscosity mu in cP or Pa s]
    L --> M[Display Analog Dial or Digital Readout]
    M --> N[Optional 4 to 20 mA or Modbus Output to IoT SCADA]
```

**Reading the diagram (left-to-right signal flow):**
- Mains power → regulated supply → synchronous motor.
- Motor drives the spindle via belt/pulley.
- The fluid resists rotation → exerts viscous torque → spring deflects → pointer moves.
- The deflection is read on the dial and converted to viscosity using the formula or factor.

## 4.2 Component-Level Cross-Section (Schematic Description)

Since a physical cross-section drawing is best, here is a **labelled ASCII-style schematic** of the bob-cup arrangement. This is what you would draw in your record under "**Setup Diagram**":

```
        +----------------------------+   <-- Step Pulley / Motor Coupling
        |        Synchronous Motor   |
        +-------------+--------------+
                      |
                      v
        +----------------------------+   <-- Top of Guard Leg
        |       Beryllium-Copper     |
        |   (Calibrated Torsion)     |
        |        SPRING              |   <-- Twists by angle θ
        +-------------+--------------+
                      |
                      v
        +----------------------------+
        |        SPINDLE SHAFT        |
        |            |                |
        |            v                |
        |       +----+----+           |
        |       |   BOB   |           |   <-- Radius R1
        |       |  (rotor)|           |   <-- Length L
        |       +----+----+           |
        |            |                |
        +------------+----------------+   <-- Bottom of Guard Leg
                     |
        ~~~~~~~~~~~~~|~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~  <-- Fluid free surface
                     |
        +------------v----------------+
        |                            |
        |     TEST FLUID (μ)         |   <-- Density ρ
        |   in SAMPLE CUP/CONTAINER   |
        |   (radius R2)              |
        +----------------------------+
```

**Key geometric labels to include in your lab diagram:**
- $R_1$ = radius of bob (spindle)
- $R_2$ = radius of cup
- $L$ = length of bob immersed
- $\omega$ = angular velocity
- $\theta$ = pointer deflection

## 4.3 Signal-Conditioning Chain (Modern IoT-Enabled Viscometer)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Spindle Torque Sensor] --> S2[Signal Amplifier]
    S2 --> S3[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    S3 --> S4[Microcontroller ESP32 or STM32]
    S4 --> S5[LCD Display Local cP Readout]
    S4 --> S6[WiFi Modbus MQTT Transmitter]
    S6 --> S7[Cloud Dashboard or SCADA HMI]
    S4 --> S8[SD Card Data Logger]
```

This IoT-enabled block chain is the **PCICL508 lab emphasis** — connecting the legacy analog sensor to a **digital acquisition and cloud-monitoring pipeline**, which is part of the Industrial Instrumentation & IoT Lab philosophy.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define dynamic viscosity and kinematic viscosity. State their SI and CGS units.**

**Model Answer:**

**Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is the property of a fluid that offers resistance to the relative motion of its layers. It is defined by Newton's law of viscosity: $\tau = \mu(du/dy)$.
- **SI unit:** Pa·s (N·s/m²)
- **CGS unit:** Poise (P); 1 P = 0.1 Pa·s; sub-unit centipoise (cP) where 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s.

**Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid: $\nu = \mu/\rho$.
- **SI unit:** m²/s
- **CGS unit:** Stokes (St); 1 St = 10⁻⁴ m²/s; sub-unit centistokes (cSt).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the principle of operation of a rotational viscometer.**

**Model Answer:**
