> ☐ **Slew rate check:** $SR_{min} = 2\pi f V_{peak}$. For 1 kHz, 1 V RMS, this is **~ 0.009 V/µs** — TL081 is fine.
>
> ☐ **Final output scaling** to **0–5 V DC** or **4–20 mA** for ADC/PLC compatibility.
>
> ☐ **Two-electrode vs four-electrode:** Two-electrode is for low to medium conductivity; four-electrode is for high conductivity or harsh chemicals.
>
> ☐ **Calibration** uses standard KCl solutions (e.g., 0.01 M KCl = **1413 µS/cm at 25 °C**).
>
> ☐ **Industrial applications:** Water treatment plants, pharmaceutical pure water, food & beverage (CIP cleaning), chemical process control, boiler feedwater monitoring, desalination, semiconductor wet-bench rinsing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control valve inherent flow characteristics (with and without positioner)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL508 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION & IOT LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Control valve inherent flow characteristics (with and without positioner)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control Valve Inherent Flow Characteristic** is the *static* relationship between the volumetric flow rate $Q$ passing through the valve and the valve stem travel (lift) $h$, expressed as a function of the **constant pressure drop** across the valve ($\Delta P_v = \text{constant}$). It represents the *manufactured* or *intrinsic* behaviour of the valve trim — independent of the process piping.

Mathematically:

$$\boxed{Q = f(h)\Big|_{\Delta P_v = \text{constant}}}$$

The *installed* characteristic, on the other hand, is what the valve exhibits *in the actual plant*, where the pressure drop across the valve varies with flow due to piping losses.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Inherent"?** The word *inherent* means "built-in". The characteristic is a property of the **trim geometry** (the shape of the plug, seat, and cage) determined at the factory bench test under ANSI/ISA S75 standards with constant $\Delta P_v$.

## 1.2 The Four Standard Inherent Characteristics

| # | Characteristic | Behavioural Shape | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Linear** | $Q \propto h$ | Liquid level, simple flow loops |
| 2 | **Equal Percentage (EQ%)** | Exponential growth | Heat exchangers, pressure control |
| 3 | **Quick Opening** | High initial slope, plateau | On–off / safety / relief service |
| 4 | **Square Root / Parabolic** | $Q \propto \sqrt{h}$ | (Rare; intermediate of linear & QO) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Multi-Tap Shower" Viewpoint

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a wall-mounted shower with **three taps** controlling water to a bucket.
> - **Quick Opening tap** → twist it just 10% and the bucket fills rapidly; the remaining 90% barely adds more water.
> - **Linear tap** → the bucket fills at a *constant* rate equal to (twist angle × maximum rate).
> - **Equal Percentage tap** → the flow doubles for *every equal increment* of twist. It feels sluggish at first, but each turn of the handle dramatically multiplies the flow.

A control valve trim (plug + seat) is engineered to mimic exactly one of these behaviours by machining the plug profile (contoured plug, V-port, characterized cage, etc.).

## 1.4 Role of a Valve Positioner

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **valve positioner** is a local feedback controller that mechanically/hydraulically/pneumatically forces the valve stem to follow the controller's set-point signal with high fidelity. It closes the loop on **stem position**, eliminating dead-band, packing friction, and stem-force imbalance caused by high $\Delta P_v$.

With a positioner:

$$\text{Stem Position } h \;\equiv\; \text{Controller Output Signal}$$

Without a positioner, the relationship between signal and stem travel is *non-ideal* due to:

1. **Stem packing friction** (static + dynamic).
2. **Process fluid force** acting on the plug (unbalanced area).
3. **Pneumatic actuator dead-band** (~2–5 % of span).
4. **Hysteresis** in the diaphragm/spring assembly.

The **inherent characteristic** is *altered* by these non-linearities when no positioner is used.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Treatment of Each Inherent Characteristic

### 2.1.1 Linear Characteristic

$$Q = Q_{\max} \cdot \left(\frac{h}{H}\right)$$

- The flow varies **directly proportional** to stem lift.
- Trim: a plain *cylindrical plug* (or V-port with linear taper).

### 2.1.2 Equal Percentage Characteristic

$$Q = Q_{\max} \cdot R^{\left(\frac{h/H - 1}{1}\right)}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\boxed{\frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = R^{\left(\frac{h}{H} - 1\right)}}$$

Where:
- $R$ = Rangeability = ratio of maximum controllable flow to minimum controllable flow ($R = Q_{\max}/Q_{\min}$), typically **30 : 1** to **50 : 1** for EQ% valves.
- $h/H$ = fractional stem travel (0 to 1).
- The *increment* in flow for an equal increment of lift is **proportional to the flow itself**:
$$\frac{dQ}{dh} = \frac{\ln(R)}{H}\cdot Q$$

This is why EQ% gives constant *gain* relative to flow — perfect for processes whose gain varies with throughput.

### 2.1.3 Quick Opening Characteristic

$$Q = Q_{\max} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{h}{H}}$$

- Achieved with a *flat* or *disc-shaped* plug.
- Maximum flow is reached at ~20–30 % stem travel.

### 2.1.4 Square Root / Parabolic

$$Q = Q_{\max} \cdot \left(\frac{h}{H}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}$$

## 2.2 Why EQ% is the Industry Workhorse

> [!NOTE]
> In **heat exchangers**, process gain is **inversely proportional** to flow ($K_p \propto 1/Q$). A linear valve has *constant* installed gain — combined with decreasing $K_p$, the loop gain falls off at high flow, causing sluggish control. The EQ% valve's *increasing* gain exactly compensates, yielding a nearly constant loop gain across the operating range.

## 2.3 Effect of Positioner on Characteristic — The Key Lab Observation

| Parameter | Without Positioner | With Positioner |
|---|---|---|
| Stem vs. signal relationship | Non-linear, with **dead-band** | **Linear**, 1:1 tracking |
| Hysteresis | 2–5 % of span | < 0.5 % of span |
| Characteristic distortion | Significant at low lift | **Negligible** — inherent curve is preserved |
| Stem response speed | Slow (5–15 s) | Fast (< 1 s) |
| Useful for high $\Delta P$ service | No (stem stalls) | **Yes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lab Outcome (Experiment 2):** When you plot $Q$ vs. $h$ in the lab, the **without-positioner** curve will be *shifted* and show a *dead-zone* near the lower travel region, deviating from the manufacturer's published inherent curve. With a positioner, the experimental curve **overlaps** the published inherent curve, confirming the positioner's role in *revealing* the true inherent characteristic.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Note:** Vertical bars inside math expressions are written as `\vert` to keep the markdown table valid. Do **NOT** use `|` in equation columns.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $Q$ | Volumetric flow through valve | $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ |
| $Q_{\max}$ | Maximum flow at full lift ($h = H$) | $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ |
| $Q_{\min}$ | Minimum controllable flow | $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ |
| $h$ | Actual stem lift | $\text{mm}$ |
| $H$ | Maximum rated stem travel | $\text{mm}$ |
| $R$ | Rangeability $= Q_{\max}/Q_{\min}$ | $30$ to $50$ (EQ%) |
| $K_v$ | Valve flow coefficient (metric) | $\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ at $1\,\text{bar}$ |
| $C_v$ | Valve flow coefficient (US) | $\text{US gal/min}$ at $1\,\text{psi}$ |
| $\Delta P_v$ | Pressure drop across valve | $\text{bar}$ |
| $\beta$ | Trim characteristic parameter | unitless |
| $K_v$ relation | $K_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\rho / \Delta P_v}$ | — |

The **valve flow coefficient** ties characteristic to sizing:

$$Q = K_v \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_v}{\rho}}$$

## 2.5 Valve Gain

The **inherent valve gain** is the slope of the characteristic curve at any operating point:

$$K_v(h) = \frac{dQ}{dh}$$

| Characteristic | $K_v(h)$ Behaviour |
|---|---|
| Linear | **Constant** |
| EQ% | **Exponentially increasing** with $h$ |
| Quick Opening | **Exponentially decreasing** with $h$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exam:** *Linear = Level plateau*, *EQ% = Exponential up*, *QO = Quick up then plateau*. The letters **L–E–Q** read top-to-bottom in alphabetical order match the curve shape progressions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Linear Characteristic from First Principles

**Given:** The trim is a cylindrical plug moving through a circular seat. The annular area $A$ exposed to flow is:

$$A(h) = \pi \cdot d \cdot h$$

where $d$ is the **seat diameter** and $h$ is the lift. The instantaneous flow velocity (Bernoulli, with constant $\Delta P_v$) is:

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P_v}{\rho}}$$

Therefore, the volumetric flow rate is:

$$Q = A \cdot v = \pi d h \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2\Delta P_v}{\rho}}$$

Define a *valve constant* $K = \pi d \sqrt{2\Delta P_v/\rho}$. Then:

$$Q = K \cdot h$$

Normalising between $(0, Q_{\max})$ and $(0, H)$:

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = \frac{h}{H}$$

This is the **linear inherent characteristic** — a *straight line* through the origin with slope $Q_{\max}/H$. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation — Equal Percentage Characteristic

**Starting premise:** *Equal increments of stem travel must produce equal percentage changes in flow rate.* That is:

$$\frac{dQ/Q}{dh} = \alpha \quad \text{(a constant)}$$

**Step 1.** Separate variables:

$$\frac{dQ}{Q} = \alpha \, dh$$

**Step 2.** Integrate from $h = 0$ (where $Q = Q_{\min}$) to an arbitrary $h$ (where $Q$ is the flow):

$$\int_{Q_{\min}}^{Q} \frac{dQ'}{Q'} = \int_{0}^{h} \alpha \, dh'$$

**Step 3.** Evaluate the integrals:

$$\ln Q - \ln Q_{\min} = \alpha h$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{Q}{Q_{\min}}\right) = \alpha h$$

**Step 4.** Exponentiate both sides:

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{\min}} = e^{\alpha h}$$

**Step 5.** At maximum lift $h = H$, the flow is $Q_{\max}$. The rangeability is $R = Q_{\max}/Q_{\min}$, so:

$$\frac{Q_{\max}}{Q_{\min}} = e^{\alpha H} = R$$

$$\therefore \alpha = \frac{\ln R}{H}$$

**Step 6.** Substitute back to obtain the canonical EQ% form:

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{\min}} = e^{\left(\frac{h}{H}\right)\ln R} = R^{\,h/H}$$

**Step 7.** Normalise between 0 and 1 by using $Q_{\max}$:

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = \frac{R^{\,h/H}}{R} = R^{\,(h/H) - 1}$$

**Final canonical equation:**

$$\boxed{\frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = R^{\,(h/H) - 1}}$$

For example, with $R = 50$ at $h/H = 0.5$:

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = 50^{\,0.5 - 1} = 50^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50}} \approx 0.1414$$

So at 50 % lift, only ~14 % of max flow is delivered — the hallmark sluggish start of EQ%. $\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation — Quick Opening Characteristic

**Premise:** The exposed area $A$ increases *faster* than linearly, e.g., $A \propto \sqrt{h}$ for a *poppet* (disc-on-seat) geometry. Using the same Bernoulli framework as §3.1:

$$Q = \pi d \cdot \sqrt{h \cdot H_{\text{ref}}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2\Delta P_v}{\rho}}$$

Combine constants into $K'$:

$$Q = K' \sqrt{h}$$

Normalise:

$$\frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = \sqrt{\frac{h}{H}} = \left(\frac{h}{H}\right)^{1/2} \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.4 Derivation — Installed Gain of an EQ% Valve Compensating a Heat Exchanger

**Process model:** Heat exchanger outlet temperature $T$ vs. steam flow $Q$ obeys:

$$K_p = \frac{\partial T}{\partial Q} = \frac{K_0}{Q} \quad \text{(inverse gain)}$$

**Valve gain (EQ%):** From §3.2, $\displaystyle \frac{dQ}{dh} = \frac{\ln R}{H} \cdot Q$.

**Loop gain** (open-loop process) becomes:

$$K_{\text{loop}} = K_p \cdot \frac{dQ}{dh} = \frac{K_0}{Q} \cdot \frac{\ln R}{H} \cdot Q = \frac{K_0 \ln R}{H}$$

The $Q$ terms **cancel** exactly, producing a *constant* loop gain — the elegant reason EQ% valves are paired with integrating / non-linear processes. $\blacksquare$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix Summary

| # | Characteristic | Normalised $Q$ vs $h/H$ | Gain $dQ/d(h/H)$ | Trim Geometry |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear | $h/H$ | $Q_{\max}$ | Cylindrical plug |
| 2 | EQ% ($R=50$) | $50^{(h/H)-1}$ | $Q_{\max} \cdot \ln 50 \cdot R^{(h/H)-1}$ | Contoured / characterized cage |
| 3 | Quick Opening | $\sqrt{h/H}$ | $0.5\,Q_{\max}/\sqrt{h/H}$ | Flat poppet / disc |
| 4 | Square root | $(h/H)^{1/2}$ | $0.5\,Q_{\max}(h/H)^{-1/2}$ | Special parabolic trim |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Control Valve Assembly with Positioner

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ctrl[Controller Output\n3-15 psi or 4-20 mA] --> posIn{Positioner\nInput}
    posIn --> posFB[Positioner\nPI Algorithm]
    posFB --> amp[Pneumatic Relay /\nCurrent to Pressure]
    amp --> act[Diaphragm\nActuator]
    act --> stem[Valve Stem\nLift h]
    stem --> trim[Valve Trim\nPlug + Seat]
    trim --> flow[Process Flow Q]
    stem -.->|Stem Position\nFeedback| posFB
    posIn2[Set Point\nSignal] --> posIn

    classDef io fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#997404,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000
    classDef proc fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000
    class ctrl,posIn,posFB,amp,act,stem,trim ctrl
    class flow,posIn2 io
    class ctrl proc
```

## 4.2 Characteristic Curves — Comparative Plot (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SC[Inherent Flow Characteristics: Q-Qmax vs h-H]
        direction LR
        ax["Stem Travel  h/H  →\n0              0.5              1.0"]
        L["LINEAR\nStraight line\nslope = 1.0"]
        EQ["EQUAL PERCENTAGE\nR = 50\nCurves up — slow start\nfast finish"]
        QO["QUICK OPENING\nSaturates early\nplateau near 0.3 lift"]
        SQ["SQUARE ROOT\nParabolic\nlies between L and QO"]
    end
    ax --> L
    ax --> EQ
    ax --> QO
    ax --> SQ

    classDef axfill fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
    classDef lin fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000
    classDef eqp fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#997404,color:#000
    classDef qop fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000
    classDef sqp fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000
    class ax axfill
    class L lin
    class EQ eqp
    class QO qop
    class SQ sqp
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Lab Setup for Experiment 2

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Air Supply\n3-15 psi regulated] --> B[Pneumatic Controller\nor Manual I/P Converter]
    B --> C[Signal Line to Valve]
    C --> D{Positioner?\nYES or NO}
    D -- YES --> E[Positioner Active\nStem tracks signal 1:1]
    D -- NO --> F[Direct to Actuator\nDead-band present]
    E --> G[Pneumatic Actuator\nDiaphragm + Spring]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Stem Lift h\nmeasured by LVDT]
    H --> I[Valve Trim\nPlug in Seat]
    I --> J[Rotameter / Orifice\nFlow Q measurement]
    J --> K[Plot Q vs h\nCompare with catalogue]

    classDef sup fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#055160,color:#000
    classDef ctl fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#997404,color:#000
    classDef opt fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
    classDef act fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000
    classDef meas fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000
    class A sup
    class B,C ctl
    class D opt
    class E,F,G act
    class H,I,J,K meas
```

## 4.4 Positioner Effect on Characteristic — Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph WOP[WITHOUT Positioner]
        W1[Input: 3 psi] --> W2[Stem: 5% lift\ndead band]
        W2 --> W3[Distorted curve\nQ lags signal]
    end
    subgraph WP[WITH Positioner]
        P1[Input: 3 psi] --> P2[Stem: 12% lift\nlinear tracking]
        P2 --> P3[True inherent curve\nQ matches catalog]
    end

    classDef wop fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000
    classDef wp fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000
    class W1,W2,W3 wop
    class P1,P2,P3 wp
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The lab block diagram (4.3) is the canonical "what you wire up" picture for the viva. The "WITHOUT vs WITH Positioner" comparison (4.4) is the *direct experimental evidence* you will quote in your record.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define "inherent flow characteristic" of a control valve. Why is it called "inherent"?*

**Model Answer:**

The inherent flow characteristic of a control valve is the static relationship between the volumetric flow rate $Q$ through the valve and the stem lift $h$, evaluated under the **laboratory condition of constant pressure drop** $\Delta P_v$ across the valve. It is represented as $Q = f(h)$ at $\Delta P_v = \text{constant}$.

It is termed *inherent* because the characteristic is an *intrinsic* property of the **valve trim geometry** — the shape of the plug, seat, and cage — determined at the manufacturer's bench test. It is independent of the connected piping system and is therefore reproducible for any two identical valves, making it a *factory-defined* behavioural fingerprint of the trim. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*List the four standard inherent characteristics. State one typical industrial application of the equal-percentage characteristic.*

**Model Answer:**

The four standard inherent characteristics are:

1. **Linear** — $Q \propto h$
2. **Equal Percentage (EQ%)** — $Q = Q_{\max} \cdot R^{(h/H) - 1}$
3. **Quick Opening** — $Q \propto \sqrt{h}$
4. **Square Root / Parabolic** — $Q \propto (h)^{1/2}$

A typical industrial application of the **equal-percentage characteristic** is in **heat-exchanger temperature control loops**, where the process gain varies inversely with the manipulated flow. The increasing gain of the EQ% valve compensates the decreasing process gain, yielding a near-constant loop gain and uniform control quality across the operating range. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students answer **one full question** of 14 marks per module. Two alternatives are provided — answer *either* Question A *or* Question B.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Modified]`

#### (a) [7 Marks]
*With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the function of a valve positioner. How does it modify the effective characteristic of a control valve?*

#### (b) [7 Marks]
*A pneumatic control valve has a linear inherent characteristic. The maximum flow is $Q_{\max} = 100\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ at full lift $H = 20\,\text{mm}$. Compute the flow at lifts of (i) $h = 4\,\text{mm}$ and (ii) $h = 12\,\text{mm}$. Also find the inherent valve gain $K_v = dQ/dh$ in each case. Comment on the result.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Function of a Valve Positioner [7 marks]**

A valve positioner is a local closed-loop controller that receives the controller's pneumatic (3–15 psi) or current (4–20 mA) signal and **forces the valve stem to track that signal precisely** by comparing the desired position with the actual stem position obtained from a feedback linkage (lever + LVDT or pneumatic pilot).

**Block diagram:**

```
Set-Point Signal ──► [∑] ──► [Pneumatic Relay] ──► [Actuator] ──► [Stem + Valve]
                       ▲                                                      
                       │                                                      
                  [Position Feedback]  ◄──────────── Lever / LVDT
```

**Functions performed:**

1. **Eliminates dead-band** caused by stem packing friction (typically 2–5 %).
2. **Compensates process forces** (unbalanced plug area) at high $\Delta P_v$.
3. **Improves dynamic response** — stem slewing rate goes from ~5 s to < 1 s.
4. **Provides split-range or reverse-acting** capability for cascaded valves.
5. **Preserves the inherent characteristic** — without it, the actual $Q$–$h$ curve deviates from the manufacturer's published curve, especially at low lifts.

Because of (5), the positioner *reveals* the true inherent characteristic; without it, the effective (installed) characteristic is *distorted* by non-linearities and the loop gain becomes unpredictable. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Given:** Linear inherent characteristic, $Q_{\max} = 100\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$, $H = 20\,\text{mm}$.

**Linear equation:**

$$Q = Q_{\max} \cdot \frac{h}{H} = 100 \cdot \frac{h}{20} = 5h \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{h}]$$

**(i) At $h = 4\,\text{mm}$:**

$$Q_1 = 5 \times 4 = 20\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**(ii) At $h = 12\,\text{mm}$:**

$$Q_2 = 5 \times 12 = 60\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Inherent valve gain (slope):**

For a linear characteristic,

$$K_v = \frac{dQ}{dh} = \frac{Q_{\max}}{H} = \frac{100}{20} = 5\,\text{m}^3/\text{h per mm}$$

Hence, $K_v$ at $h = 4\,\text{mm}$ is $5\,\text{m}^3/\text{h/mm}$ and at $h = 12\,\text{mm}$ is **also** $5\,\text{m}^3/\text{h/mm}$.

**Comment:** The gain of a *linear* valve is **constant** at $5\,\text{m}^3/\text{h/mm}$ regardless of the operating point. This is a defining feature — the linear trim delivers uniform control resolution across its travel. However, in processes whose gain decreases with flow (e.g., heat exchangers), this constant valve gain cannot compensate, so an EQ% trim is preferred. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

#### (a) [7 Marks]
*Derive the mathematical expression for the equal-percentage inherent characteristic, starting from the defining premise that equal increments of stem travel produce equal percentage changes in flow. State clearly what "rangeability" means.*

#### (b) [7 Marks]
*An EQ% valve has $Q_{\max} = 120\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$, $Q_{\min} = 4\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ and maximum stem lift $H = 25\,\text{mm}$. Calculate: (i) the rangeability $R$, (ii) the flow $Q$ at $h = 5\,\text{mm}$, $15\,\text{mm}$ and $25\,\text{mm}$, (iii) the valve gain $dQ/dh$ at $h = 15\,\text{mm}$.*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of EQ% Characteristic [7 marks]**

**Premise:** Equal increments of stem lift $dh$ cause equal *percentage* changes in flow $dQ/Q$:

$$\frac{dQ}{Q} = \alpha \, dh \quad (\alpha = \text{constant})$$

Separating and integrating from $h=0, Q=Q_{\min}$ to $h, Q$:

$$\int_{Q_{\min}}^{Q} \frac{dQ'}{Q'} = \int_{0}^{h} \alpha \, dh'$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{Q}{Q_{\min}}\right) = \alpha h$$

$$\boxed{\frac{Q}{Q_{\min}} = e^{\alpha h}}$$

At $h = H$, $Q = Q_{\max}$, and **rangeability** is defined as:

$$R = \frac{Q_{\max}}{Q_{\min}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = e^{\alpha H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{\ln R}{H}$$

Hence the canonical form is:

$$\boxed{\frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = R^{\,(h/H) - 1}}$$

**Rangeability** $R$ is the ratio of the *maximum controllable* flow to the *minimum controllable* flow of the trim. For a typical EQ% plug, $R$ lies between 30 and 50. **(7 marks)**

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 marks]**

**Given:** $Q_{\max} = 120\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$, $Q_{\min} = 4\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$, $H = 25\,\text{mm}$.

**(i) Rangeability:**

$$R = \frac{Q_{\max}}{Q_{\min}} = \frac{120}{4} = 30$$

**(ii) Flow at various lifts** using $\displaystyle \frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = R^{(h/H)-1}$:

**At $h = 5\,\text{mm}$:** $h/H = 0.2$

$$Q_1 = 120 \times 30^{\,(0.2 - 1)} = 120 \times 30^{-0.8}$$

Compute $30^{-0.8} = e^{-0.8 \ln 30} = e^{-0.8 \times 3.4012} = e^{-2.7210} = 0.0658$

$$Q_1 = 120 \times 0.0658 \approx 7.90\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**At $h = 15\,\text{mm}$:** $h/H = 0.6$

$$Q_2 = 120 \times 30^{\,(0.6 - 1)} = 120 \times 30^{-0.4}$$

Compute $30^{-0.4} = e^{-0.4 \times 3.4012} = e^{-1.3605} = 0.2565$

$$Q_2 = 120 \times 0.2565 \approx 30.78\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**At $h = 25\,\text{mm}$:** $h/H = 1.0$

$$Q_3 = 120 \times 30^{\,0} = 120 \times 1 = 120\,\text{m}^3/\text{h} \;\checkmark$$

**(iii) Valve gain at $h = 15\,\text{mm}$:**

For an EQ% valve, $dQ/dh = (\ln R / H) \cdot Q$:

$$\left.\frac{dQ}{dh}\right|_{h=15} = \frac{\ln 30}{25} \times 30.78 = \frac{3.4012}{25} \times 30.78 = 0.1360 \times 30.78 \approx 4.19\,\text{m}^3/\text{h per mm}$$

**Comment:** Compare with §3.4 — the gain increases *exponentially* with lift. At low lift the gain is small, at high lift the gain is large. This is precisely the property that compensates processes whose gain *decreases* with flow. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $-1$ exponent in EQ%** — Students commonly write $Q/Q_{\max} = R^{h/H}$, which is wrong. The correct form is $R^{(h/H - 1)}$ so that $Q = Q_{\max}$ at $h = H$ and $Q = Q_{\max}/R$ at $h = 0$.
> 2. **Mixing inherent and installed characteristics** — A 14-mark question that asks for "characteristic" in a *plant* context is asking for the *installed* characteristic. Read the question stem carefully.
> 3. **Ignoring units of $K_v$** — Always write $\text{m}^3/\text{h per mm}$ (or equivalent) in your answer. Marks are deducted for naked numerical slopes.
> 4. **Positioner not a controller** — A positioner is a *local feedback* device, not a process controller. Examiners deduct marks if students confuse the two.
> 5. **Hysteresis in plots** — In lab records, students often draw a *single* line for the without-positioner case. The correct plot should show **two slightly different curves** for increasing and decreasing lifts, indicating hysteresis.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Inherent characteristic** = $Q$ vs $h$ at **constant $\Delta P_v$**, an intrinsic property of the **trim geometry**.
- **Installed characteristic** = $Q$ vs $h$ in the **actual piping**, where $\Delta P_v$ drops with flow due to pipe friction.
- **Four standard types:** Linear, Equal Percentage, Quick Opening, Square Root.
- **Linear:** $Q/Q_{\max} = h/H$, **constant** gain.
- **Equal Percentage:** $Q/Q_{\max} = R^{(h/H) - 1}$, **exponentially increasing** gain; rangeability $R = 30$–$50$.
- **Quick Opening:** $Q/Q_{\max} = \sqrt{h/H}$, **decreasing** gain; reaches $Q_{\max}$ at ~30 % lift.
- **Rangeability** $R = Q_{\max}/Q_{\min}$.
- **Valve gain** $K_v = dQ/dh$, units: $\text{m}^3/\text{h per mm}$.
- **Positioner** = local closed-loop on stem position; removes dead-band, friction and stem-force errors.
- **Without positioner:** dead-band 2–5 %, characteristic distorted at low lift, stem response slow.
- **With positioner:** 1:1 stem tracking, < 0.5 % hysteresis, true inherent curve revealed.
- **EQ% + heat exchanger** = constant loop gain — the classic pairing.
- **Valve flow coefficient** $K_v = Q \sqrt{\rho / \Delta P_v}$, used for **sizing**, not for the characteristic itself.
- **Trim shapes** you must remember: *cylindrical plug* (linear), *contoured plug / characterized cage* (EQ%), *flat poppet* (quick opening).
- **Lab experiment tip:** Always record *two* runs (increasing and decreasing lift) to capture hysteresis; plot both on the same graph for the **without-positioner** case.
- **Viva tip:** Be ready to state why a linear valve is unsuitable for an integrating non-linear process — quote the loop-gain cancellation argument in §3.4.
- **Bench-top constants to memorise:** $3$–$15\,\text{psi}$ (pneumatic standard), $4$–$20\,\text{mA}$ (electronic standard), $20$–$100\,\text{kPa}$ (SI pneumatic).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of the characteristics of elements of final control operation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCICL508 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION & IOT LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Study of the characteristics of elements of final control operation ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a "Final Control Element"? In any closed-loop industrial process control system (such as the classical Error Detector → Controller → Final Control Element → Process → Sensor feedback loop), the **Final Control Element (FCE)** is the physical hardware component that *directly* manipulates the process variable by altering the mass/energy flow into the process, in response to the controller's corrective signal. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Coughanowr & Cooper Standard):** A final control element is the device that *physically implements* the control action determined by the controller. It is the "muscle" of the control loop, receiving a low-power signal (3–15 psi pneumatic, 4–20 mA electronic, or 0–10 V digital) and converting it into a mechanical displacement that changes the manipulated variable flow. The three primary families of FCEs studied in PCICL508 Lab are: | FCE Type | Manipulated Variable | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Pneumatic Control Valve** | Fluid flow rate | Chemical reactors, oil refineries | | **Electric Actuator / Motor Drive** | Shaft speed / position | Conveyor belts, mixers, fans | | **Damper / Louver** | Air/gas flow area | HVAC, furnace draft control | ### 1.2 The Control Valve — King of Final Control Elements Among all FCEs, the **Globe-type Control Valve with Pneumatic Diaphragm Actuator** is the most widely used (over 70% of industrial loops). It is therefore the centerpiece of this experiment. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Faucet-Thermostat" Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a domestic shower system. The cold/hot faucet (the **valve plug**) regulates water flow. Your hand turns the faucet handle (the **actuator**). The thermostat on the wall (the **controller**) tells you "make it warmer" — you then turn the handle a bit more or less. Now, observe: when the handle is at 10% rotation, almost no water comes out. At 50%, a moderate stream. At 90%, a torrent. This non-linear relationship between *handle position* and *water flow* is precisely the **inherent flow characteristic** of the valve — the very thing we are going to "study" in this experiment. In an industrial plant, instead of your hand, a **diaphragm actuator** pushes the valve stem, and instead of a thermostat, a **PID controller** issues the command signal. ### 1.4 Components of a Pneumatic Control Valve Assembly A complete final control operation assembly consists of three sub-systems: 1. **Actuator** — Converts the controller signal (e.g., 3–15 psi) into mechanical thrust. 2. **Valve Body (with Trim)** — Actually throttles the fluid flow. 3. **Valve Positioner (optional but common)** — A local feedback loop ensuring the stem actually reaches the demanded position despite friction, packing, or process pressure disturbances. > [!TIP] > **Key Concept:** The lab experiment typically uses a bench-top test rig where a pneumatic diaphragm actuator (with 3–15 psi input) drives a single-seat globe valve, and students record (a) Stem position vs. signal pressure, and (b) Flow rate vs. stem position at constant upstream/downstream pressure drop. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Anatomy of a Pneumatic Diaphragm Actuator When compressed air at pressure **P** (3–15 psi) is admitted into the upper chamber above a flexible elastomer/rubber diaphragm of effective area **A**, it produces a downward thrust: $$F_{thrust} = P \times A - F_{spring}$$ The thrust moves the **valve stem** downward against the **return spring** (which provides fail-safe action on air failure). The spring force **F_spring = K × x**, where **x** is the stem displacement. Equilibrium is reached when thrust = spring force. ### 2.2 The Three Inherent Flow Characteristics (The Heart of the Experiment) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Topic:** The *inherent* characteristic is defined as the relationship between **valve stem travel (l)** and **flow rate (Q)** when the **pressure drop across the valve is held constant** (ΔP = constant). The three standardized profiles, defined by ISA / IEC 60534, are: #### (a) Quick-Opening Characteristic A large change in flow occurs for a small initial stem movement; the curve is concave-up. Used for **on-off** or **safety** applications. $$Q / Q_{max} = \sqrt{l / l_{max}}$$ #### (b) Linear Characteristic Flow is **directly proportional** to stem travel. Equal increments of stem travel produce equal increments of flow. $$Q / Q_{max} = l / l_{max}$$ #### (c) Equal-Percentage Characteristic The **most widely used** in process control. Equal increments of stem travel produce equal *percentage* changes in the *existing* flow. The curve is concave-down on linear axes but becomes a straight line on a semi-log plot. $$Q / Q_{max} = R^{\left( l / l_{max} - 1 \right)}$$ where **R** is the **rangeability** (typically R = 30 to 50). ### 2.3 Valve Capacity Coefficient (C_v) The **C_v** (or **K_v** in metric units) is the most fundamental sizing parameter in valve selection. It is defined as: > The number of US gallons per minute of water at 60 °F that will flow through the valve with a **pressure drop of 1 psi** across it. For liquids (general sizing equation): $$C_v = \frac{Q \sqrt{G_f}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}$$ For gases and vapors (simplified form): $$C_v = \frac{Q}{N \cdot P_1} \sqrt{\frac{G_g \cdot T \cdot Z}{\Delta P \cdot (P_1 + P_2)}}$$ ### 2.4 Rangeability and Turn-Down $$R = \frac{C_{v, max}}{C_{v, min}}$$ A linear valve typically has R ≈ 30, while an equal-percentage valve offers R ≈ 50 to 100, making it preferable where the process operating point must vary widely. ### 2.5 Installed vs. Inherent Characteristic In a real piping system, the **pump head curve** interacts with the valve curve. The **installed characteristic** (what the loop actually "sees") differs from the inherent one — equal-percentage valves tend to linearize when installed, while linear valves may distort toward quick-opening behavior. This is a classic KTU viva question. ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Actuator Thrust | $F = P \cdot A - K \cdot x$ | F in N, P in Pa, A in m² | | 2 | Linear Characteristic | $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{l}{l_{max}}$ | Straight line through origin | | 3 | Equal-% Characteristic | $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{\left( l/l_{max} - 1 \right)}$ | $R \approx 30\text{–}50$ | | 4 | Quick-Opening | $\dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{l}{l_{max}}}$ | Concave-up curve | | 5 | Valve C_v (liquid) | $C_v = \dfrac{Q \sqrt{G_f}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}$ | Q in GPM, ΔP in psi | | 6 | Rangeability | $R = \dfrac{C_{v,max}}{C_{v,min}}$ | Dimensionless | | 7 | Valve Gain (linear) | $K_v = \dfrac{Q_{max}}{l_{max}}$ | Constant | | 8 | Valve Gain (equal-%) | $K_v = \dfrac{\ln(R)}{l_{max}} \cdot Q$ | Variable with Q | | 9 | Stem Position Gain | $\dfrac{dl}{dP_{signal}} = \dfrac{A}{K}$ | m/Pa, linear actuator | | 10 | Installed ΔP fraction | $x = \dfrac{\Delta P_{valve}}{\Delta P_{total}}$ | Typical $x = 0.5\text{–}0.7$ | > [!NOTE] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the **valve gain** expression for equal-percentage valves, the gain is *not* constant — it is **proportional to the current flow Q**. This self-regulating behavior is precisely why equal-percentage valves are chosen for processes with wide load variations. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Force Balance on the Diaphragm Stem The pneumatic actuator is essentially a force-balance device. We derive the static relationship between signal pressure **P** and stem displacement **x**. **Step 1 — Pressure Force on Diaphragm** The controller signal (3–15 psi) enters the top chamber. The force pushing the stem down is pressure × diaphragm area. $$F_{pressure} = P \cdot A_d$$ where $A_d$ is the effective diaphragm area in m². **Step 2 — Spring Restoring Force** A helical return spring opposes motion. By Hooke's Law, the spring force is proportional to its compression **x**. $$F_{spring} = K_s \cdot x + F_0$$ where $K_s$ is the spring constant (N/m) and $F_0$ is the pre-load (spring force at x = 0). **Step 3 — Steady-State Force Balance** At equilibrium, the net force is zero (neglecting packing friction for an ideal analysis): $$P \cdot A_d = K_s \cdot x + F_0$$ **Step 4 — Solve for Stem Displacement x** $$x = \frac{A_d}{K_s} \cdot P - \frac{F_0}{K_s}$$ **Step 5 — Express in Terms of Signal Range** Define $P_{min} = 3$ psi (corresponds to $x = 0$, fully closed) and $P_{max} = 15$ psi (corresponds to $x = x_{max}$, fully open). The spring pre-load ensures $F_0 = P_{min} \cdot A_d$. Substituting: $$x = \frac{A_d}{K_s} \cdot (P - 3 \text{ psi})$$ So the **stem travels linearly** with signal pressure — this is the desired actuator behavior. The slope: $$\frac{dx}{dP} = \frac{A_d}{K_s}$$ is called the **actuator gain**. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linearizing the Equal-Percentage Characteristic The equal-percentage valve appears highly non-linear, but it is *exactly* linear on a semi-logarithmic plot. We prove this. **Step 1 — Start with the defining equation** $$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{\left( l / l_{max} - 1 \right)}$$ **Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides** $$\ln\!\left(\frac{Q}{Q_{max}}\right) = \left(\frac{l}{l_{max}} - 1\right) \ln R$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange into straight-line form $Y = mX + c$** $$\ln Q = \underbrace{\left(\frac{\ln R}{l_{max}}\right)}_{\text{slope } m} \cdot l + \underbrace{\left(\ln Q_{max} - \ln R\right)}_{\text{intercept } c}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret the slope (the valve gain)** $$\frac{d(\ln Q)}{dl} = \frac{\ln R}{l_{max}}$$ This slope is a **constant** — meaning the *relative* change in flow per unit stem travel is fixed, regardless of operating point. This is the *defining* engineering advantage. ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Working Out a Numerical Lab Example **Problem (KTU Style):** A pneumatic control valve is rated with equal-percentage trim having rangeability R = 50. At full travel $l_{max} = 25$ mm, the maximum flow is $Q_{max} = 200$ L/min. The signal pressure range is 3–15 psi. Calculate: (i) The flow when stem travel is 15 mm. (ii) The valve gain $\dfrac{dQ}{dl}$ at 15 mm travel. (iii) The signal pressure required to deliver 80 L/min. **Solution:** **(i) Flow at l = 15 mm** $$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{\left( l / l_{max} - 1 \right)} = 50^{\left( 15/25 - 1 \right)} = 50^{-0.4}$$ Computing the exponent: $$50^{-0.4} = \frac{1}{50^{0.4}} = \frac{1}{e^{0.4 \ln 50}} = \frac{1}{e^{0.4 \times 3.912}} = \frac{1}{e^{1.565}} = \frac{1}{4.781}$$ Therefore: $$Q = 200 \times \frac{1}{4.781} \approx 41.8 \text{ L/min}$$ **(ii) Valve Gain at l = 15 mm** Differentiate the inherent characteristic with respect to *l*: $$\frac{dQ}{dl} = Q_{max} \cdot R^{(l/l_{max} - 1)} \cdot \frac{\ln R}{l_{max}}$$ Substituting values: $$\frac{dQ}{dl} = 200 \times 50^{-0.4} \times \frac{\ln 50}{25}$$ We already found $200 \times 50^{-0.4} \approx 41.8$ L/min, and $\ln 50 = 3.912$, so: $$\frac{dQ}{dl} = 41.8 \times \frac{3.912}{25} \approx 6.54 \text{ L/(min·mm)}$$ **(iii) Signal Pressure for Q = 80 L/min** First find the required travel *l*. Using the characteristic equation: $$\frac{80}{200} = 0.4 = 50^{(l/25 - 1)}$$ Take the natural log: $$\ln 0.4 = \left(\frac{l}{25} - 1\right) \ln 50$$ $$-0.9163 = \left(\frac{l}{25} - 1\right) \times 3.912$$ $$\frac{l}{25} - 1 = \frac{-0.9163}{3.912} = -0.2342$$ $$l = 25 \times (1 - 0.2342) = 25 \times 0.7658 \approx 19.15 \text{ mm}$$ Stem displacement is linear with signal pressure. The fraction of travel = 19.15 / 25 = 0.766, so: $$P = 3 + 0.766 \times (15 - 3) = 3 + 9.19 = 12.19 \text{ psi}$$ > [!TIP] > **Examiner's Trick:** Many students forget that the equal-percentage characteristic must be derived from the *flow ratio* — if you skip step (i) and directly substitute into the gain formula, you will lose the conceptual link. Always show the ratio first. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Characteristics | Property | Quick-Opening | Linear | Equal-Percentage | |---|---|---|---| | Shape (linear axes) | Concave-up | Straight line | Concave-down | | Shape (semi-log axes) | Convex | Curved | **Straight line** | | Valve gain $K_v$ | Decreases sharply | **Constant** | Increases exponentially | | Rangeability | Poor (≈ 10) | Moderate (≈ 30) | Excellent (≈ 50–100) | | Best use | On-off, safety | Constant ΔP loops | Variable load, compressor control | | Sensitivity at low flow | High | Moderate | **Low (controllable)** | | Sensitivity at high flow | Low | Moderate | High | > [!NOTE] > **Why equal-percentage dominates process industries:** When the process is at low load, the small trim opening gives a *very small* flow — easy to control precisely. At high load, the large opening gives a *very large* flow — again, fine control. The relative sensitivity remains roughly constant across the entire operating range. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Final Control Element in a Closed Control Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR SP[Setpoint] --> SUM[ Summing Junction ] PV[Process Variable] --> SUM SUM --> CTRL[ PID Controller ] CTRL --> SIG[Controller Signal
3-15 psi / 4-20 mA] SIG --> ACT[Pneumatic Diaphragm
Actuator] ACT --> STEM[Valve Stem
Translates Vertically] STEM --> PLUG[Valve Plug / Trim
Throttles Flow] PLUG --> PROC[Process / Pipeline] PROC --> SENSE[Flow / Pressure
Transmitter] SENSE --> PV POS[Valve Positioner
Local Feedback] -.-> STEM POS -.-> ACT ``` ### 4.2 Physical Architecture — A Pneumatic Globe Control Valve ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TOP[Top: Actuator Assembly] A1[Yoke / Upper Housing] A2[Diaphragm / Rolling Seal] A3[Diaphragm Chamber
Receives 3-15 psi] A4[Stem Connector] A5[Spring Compressor] end subgraph MID[Middle: Stem Region] S1[Valve Stem - SS316] S2[Packing Gland - PTFE] S3[Stem Guide Bushing] end subgraph BOT[Bottom: Valve Body] B1[Bonnet Flange] B2[Seat Ring - Hard Alloy] B3[Plug and Cage - Inherent Char] B4[Inlet Port - Flanged] B5[Outlet Port - Flanged] end A3 --> A4 --> S1 --> B3 A2 --- A1 S1 --- S2 --- S3 S1 --- B1 B4 --> B3 --> B2 B3 --> B5 ``` ### 4.3 Signal-to-Flow Signal Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Controller Pressure
3-15 psi] --> A1[Gain: A divided by Ks
m per Pa] A1 --> L1[Stem Travel l
0 to 25 mm] L1 --> A2[Inherent Characteristic
Function fl of l] A2 --> Q[Volumetric Flow Q
L per min] Q --> PROC[Process Load] PROC --> PT[Process Transmitter] PT --> FB[4-20 mA Feedback] FB --> CTRL[PID Controller] CTRL --> P ``` ### 4.4 Graphical Representation — Inherent Characteristics on a Single Plot ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PLOT[Q/Qmax vs l/lmax - All Three Profiles] D1[Quick Opening
Concave up
Max flow reached early] D2[Linear
Straight diagonal
Equal increments] D3[Equal Percentage
Concave down
Slow start fast finish] end D1 --> D2 --> D3 D2 --- NOTE[Inherent characteristic is defined
at CONSTANT pressure drop] ``` ### 4.5 Lab Test Rig — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SUPPLY[Air Supply] CMP[Compressor] --> F1[Filter Regulator] --> TG1[Pressure Gauge] end TG1 --> IPI[I to P Converter
4-20 mA in] IPI --> ACT[Actuator Chamber] ACT --> STEM[Stem Position] subgraph FLOW[Flow Circuit] PUMP[Water Pump] --> V1[Manual Isolation Valve] --> TESTV[Test Control Valve] TESTV --> ROTM[Rotameter 0-200 Lph] --> V2[Throttle Valve] V2 --> TANK[Return Tank] end TESTV --- STEM PG[Pressure Gauge Upstream] --- TESTV PG2[Pressure Gauge Downstream] --- TESTV LSP[Linear Stem Position Sensor
LVDT] --- STEM DSP[Data Acquisition System] --- ROTM DSP --- PG DSP --- PG2 DSP --- LSP DSP --- IPI ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Lab Rig:** The student varies the I/P converter current (4–20 mA) in steps, notes the actuator chamber pressure (should mirror signal), the stem position (LVDT reading), the rotameter flow, and the upstream/downstream pressures. From these, both the *actuator characteristic* (pressure vs. travel) and the *inherent valve characteristic* (flow vs. travel at constant ΔP) are plotted. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2023]** *Define the term "inherent characteristic" of a control valve. List the three standard inherent characteristics specified by ISA standards.* **Model Answer:** > The *inherent characteristic* of a control valve is the functional relationship between the **volumetric flow rate (Q) through the valve** and the **valve stem travel (l)** when the **pressure drop across the valve is held constant**. > > The three ISA-standardized inherent characteristics are: > 1. **Quick-Opening** — most flow change in the first 25% of travel > 2. **Linear** — flow is directly proportional to stem travel > 3. **Equal-Percentage** — equal increments of stem travel produce equal *percentage* changes in the existing flow --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** *What is meant by the "rangeability" of a control valve? Why is it important for an equal-percentage valve?* **Model Answer:** > **Rangeability (R)** is defined as the ratio of the maximum valve flow coefficient to the minimum controllable flow coefficient: > > $$R = \frac{C_{v,\max}}{C_{v,\min}}$$ > > For an equal-percentage valve, R is typically 50:1 or higher. It is important because the *logarithmic* sensitivity remains nearly constant across the entire range — the valve can deliver fine, controllable flow at low loads and high flow at full load without losing precision. This is why equal-percentage valves are preferred for processes with **wide load variations** (e.g., refinery furnaces, compressor anti-surge loops). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **Examiner's Note (Internal Choice Mandate, KTU 2024):** Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **pneumatic diaphragm-actuated globe valve**. Derive the force-balance equation and show that the stem displacement is a linear function of the controller signal pressure. **(b) [7 Marks]** A control valve has an equal-percentage inherent characteristic with rangeability R = 30 and full-travel flow $Q_{max} = 100$ m³/h. The valve stem has total travel $l_{max} = 20$ mm, and the controller signal range is 3–15 psi (linear mapping). Calculate: (i) The flow at 40% stem travel. (ii) The valve gain $\dfrac{dQ}{dl}$ at 40% travel. (iii) The signal pressure required to achieve 20 m³/h. **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Working:** Refer to the **Physical Architecture** diagram in Section 4.2. The construction consists of: - A **diaphragm chamber** at the top with a flexible elastomer diaphragm clamped between two housing halves. - A **diaphragm stem connector** rigidly attached to the diaphragm center, transmitting thrust to the valve stem. - A **return spring** seated in the lower portion, providing fail-safe (air-to-close) action and establishing a linear spring force. - A **globe valve body** with seat, plug, and cage trim below the bonnet. **Working:** Compressed air at 3–15 psi from a PID controller (or I/P converter) enters the diaphragm chamber. The pressure acts on the diaphragm area $A_d$, producing a downward thrust $F = P \cdot A_d$. The thrust moves the stem down, throttling the fluid through the seat-plug annulus, against the spring. When the controller output drops to 3 psi, the spring pushes the stem back up to the fail-safe (fully closed) position. **Force-Balance Derivation:** At steady state (neglecting packing friction): $$P \cdot A_d = K_s \cdot x + F_{0}$$ Using the boundary condition that at $P = P_{min} = 3$ psi, $x = 0$ and $F_0 = P_{min} \cdot A_d$: $$P \cdot A_d = K_s \cdot x + P_{min} \cdot A_d$$ Solving for *x*: $$x = \frac{A_d}{K_s} \cdot (P - 3)$$ This is a **linear** relationship between stem travel *x* and signal pressure *P*, with slope $A_d / K_s$. ∎ **(b) Numerical Part:** (i) Flow at 40% travel: $l / l_{max} = 0.4$ $$\frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{\left(l/l_{max} - 1\right)} = 30^{(0.4 - 1)} = 30^{-0.6}$$ $$30^{-0.6} = e^{-0.6 \times \ln 30} = e^{-0.6 \times 3.401} = e^{-2.041} = 0.1300$$ $$Q = 100 \times 0.1300 = 13.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ (ii) Valve gain at 40% travel: $$\frac{dQ}{dl} = Q_{max} \cdot R^{(l/l_{max} - 1)} \cdot \frac{\ln R}{l_{max}}$$ $$\frac{dQ}{dl} = 100 \times 30^{-0.6} \times \frac{\ln 30}{20 \text{ mm}}$$ $$\frac{dQ}{dl} = 100 \times 0.1300 \times \frac{3.401}{20} = \frac{44.21}{20} = 2.21 \text{ m}^3/(\text{h·mm})$$ (iii) Signal pressure for $Q = 20$ m³/h: $$\frac{20}{100} = 0.2 = 30^{(l/20 - 1)}$$ $$\ln 0.2 = \left(\frac{l}{20} - 1\right) \times \ln 30$$ $$-1.6094 = \left(\frac{l}{20} - 1\right) \times 3.401$$ $$\frac{l}{20} - 1 = \frac{-1.6094}{3.401} = -0.4732$$ $$l = 20 \times (1 - 0.4732) = 20 \times 0.5268 = 10.54 \text{ mm}$$ Fraction of travel = $10.54 / 20 = 0.527$, so the signal pressure is: $$P = 3 + 0.527 \times (15 - 3) = 3 + 6.32 = 9.32 \text{ psi}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice** **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare the three inherent flow characteristics of control valves (Quick-Opening, Linear, Equal-Percentage) with suitable graphs. Discuss, with reasoning, why **equal-percentage** valves are most commonly selected for general process control applications. **(b) [7 Marks]** Define the **valve coefficient $C_v$**. A control valve is used to throttle water at 25 °C ($G_f = 1.0$) with a pressure drop $\Delta P = 15$ psi across the valve. If the required maximum flow is 250 GPM, determine: (i) the required $C_v$, (ii) the $C_v$ at 50% stem travel assuming an equal-percentage characteristic with R = 50, (iii) the resulting flow at 50% travel. **Model Solution:** **(a) Comparative Discussion:** The three inherent characteristics are plotted on the axes of $Q/Q_{max}$ versus $l/l_{max}$ at constant ΔP. **Quick-Opening** shows a steep initial rise and quickly saturates — used for on-off and safety isolation. **Linear** provides a constant slope, but its *valve gain* (slope) is fixed — meaning it gives the *same control authority* at low flow as at high flow, which is poor for wide-range processes. **Equal-Percentage** has a low initial slope that grows exponentially, giving **small, controllable flows at small openings** and **large flows at large openings** — its *relative* sensitivity $\dfrac{d(\ln Q)}{dl}$ is constant. **Why equal-percentage dominates:** In real processes, the operating point shifts with production rate. The equal-percentage characteristic matches the logarithmic response of many processes (pH, composition) and maintains controllability across decades of flow — making it the universal choice for refinery, petrochemical, and power plant applications. ∎ **(b) $C_v$ Calculations:** (i) Required $C_v$ at maximum flow: $$C_v = \frac{Q \sqrt{G_f}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}} = \frac{250 \times \sqrt{1.0}}{\sqrt{15}} = \frac{250}{3.873} \approx 64.55$$ (ii) $C_v$ at 50% travel with equal-% and R = 50: For an equal-percentage valve, the $C_v$ ratio follows the same exponential form: $$\frac{C_v}{C_{v,\max}} = R^{(l/l_{max} - 1)} = 50^{(0.5 - 1)} = 50^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{50}} = 0.1414$$ $$C_v = 64.55 \times 0.1414 \approx 9.13$$ (iii) Flow at 50% travel: $$Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P / G_f} = 9.13 \times \sqrt{15} = 9.13 \times 3.873 \approx 35.4 \text{ GPM}$$ (Consistent with $Q = 250 \times 0.1414 = 35.4$ GPM. ✓) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting Units:** Many students write $C_v$ values without mentioning the units (GPM/√psi for US units). Always state the unit system you are using. > 2. **Mixing Inherent and Installed Characteristics:** The lab-measured curve is the *inherent* one (constant ΔP). The installed characteristic is different and applies to actual process operation. Conflating them is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. > 3. **Sign Error in Equal-Percentage Exponent:** A very common mistake is writing $R^{(1 - l/l_{max})}$ instead of $R^{(l/l_{max} - 1)}$. Remember: at $l = l_{max}$, the ratio must equal **1**, not R. > 4. **Skipping the Force-Balance Boundary Condition:** When deriving the actuator equation, students often forget to apply the spring pre-load condition $F_0 = P_{min} \cdot A_d$, leading to an answer that doesn't pass through $x = 0$ at 3 psi. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** - ☐ A **Final Control Element (FCE)** is the "muscle" of a control loop — it physically changes the manipulated variable. - ☐ The three sub-systems of a control-valve assembly: **Actuator + Valve Body + Positioner**. - ☐ The **pneumatic diaphragm actuator** operates on a force-balance between air pressure thrust and return-spring force. - ☐ Stem displacement is **linearly** proportional to controller signal pressure (3–15 psi). - ☐ The **three inherent characteristics** at constant ΔP: Quick-Opening, Linear, Equal-Percentage. - ☐ Equal-percentage equation: $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(l/l_{max} - 1)}$ — always plot on **semi-log axes** to get a straight line. - ☐ **Rangeability R** = $C_{v,max} / C_{v,min}$, typically 30 (linear) to 50+ (equal-%). - ☐ **C_v** (valve coefficient) is the flow in GPM of water at 1 psi pressure drop. - ☐ Liquid sizing: $C_v = Q \sqrt{G_f / \Delta P}$. - ☐ The **installed** characteristic differs from the **inherent** one — it depends on the ratio of valve ΔP to system ΔP. - ☐ **Equal-percentage valves are preferred** for processes with wide load variations because the relative valve gain is constant. - ☐ A **valve positioner** is a local PID loop that ensures the stem actually reaches the demanded position, overcoming packing friction and process pressure disturbances. - ☐ Key laboratory observations to record: actuator pressure, stem travel (via LVDT), rotameter flow, upstream and downstream pressures. - ☐ Common lab plots: (1) Signal pressure vs. stem position, (2) Flow vs. stem position at constant ΔP — these reveal the actuator linearity and the inherent valve characteristic.

topics.title: Study of transmitter characteristics of level, flow, temperature, and pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL508 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION & IOT LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of transmitter characteristics of level, flow, temperature, and pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Transmitter?

A **transmitter** is an electronic device used in industrial process control that **senses a physical process variable** (level, flow, temperature, pressure) via a primary sensor element, **converts it into a proportional, standardized electrical signal** (most commonly a **4–20 mA current loop** or a **0–10 V voltage signal**), and transmits this signal over long distances to controllers, recorders, indicators, or PLCs (Programmable Logic Controllers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (Exact Terminology):**
> A transmitter is a transducer device that **converts the output of a sensor into a standardized signal** (4–20 mA, 0–10 V, or 0–5 V) that is proportional to the measured process variable and capable of being transmitted over long distances with **negligible signal degradation**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Standardization?**
> Every sensor produces a different raw signal (mV, Ω, pF, Hz). The transmitter normalizes this to a **universal industrial standard**, so that the same controller/indicator can accept inputs from **any sensor type or manufacturer**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator at an International Conference"

Imagine you are at a global conference where speakers from 50 different countries (Japan, France, Brazil, India…) are talking. Each speaks a different native language. To make them all understood by the audience, you hire a **team of translators**. Each translator:
1. **Listens** to one specific speaker (the **sensor**).
2. **Understands** the meaning (the **signal conditioning**).
3. **Speaks** in **one common language** — say, English — to the audience (the **controller/indicator**).

A transmitter does exactly this in an industrial plant:
- **Sensor** = Native speaker (thermocouple, RTD, strain gauge).
- **Signal conditioner** = Brain of the translator (amplifier, linearizer).
- **Transmitter output** = Common standardized electrical language (**4–20 mA**).
- **Receiver** = The audience (PLC, DCS, recorder).

---

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Output Signals

| Signal Type | Range | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Current Loop (Pneumo-Electric Standard)** | **4–20 mA DC** | Industry gold standard; live-zero (4 mA) detects wire break |
| Voltage (Low-level) | 0–5 V DC | Used in panel meters, short distances |
| Voltage (High-level) | 0–10 V DC | Used in PLC analog cards, medium distance |
| Pneumatic (Legacy) | 3–15 psi | Used in older oil & gas plants |
| Digital Smart Protocol | HART, Foundation Fieldbus, Profibus PA | Modern two-way digital communication |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Memory Trick — "4 mA = Alive, 0 mA = Dead":**
> The **4 mA** lower limit (called the *live zero*) allows the controller to distinguish a **true 0% reading** from a **broken wire** (which would read 0 mA). This is a **2-mark favourite** in KTU exams.

---

## 1.4 Why Study Transmitter Characteristics?

In an industrial plant, the transmitter is often **the only link** between the physical process and the control room. If the transmitter is inaccurate, the entire control loop is inaccurate. Hence KTU emphasizes the study of:

- **Span, Zero, Range**
- **Linearity**
- **Accuracy**
- **Hysteresis**
- **Repeatability**
- **Dead Band**
- **Sensitivity / Resolution**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CO Mapping (KTU 2024 Scheme):** This experiment is mapped to **CO2: Analyze the performance characteristics of industrial process transmitters**, Bloom's Level: **Apply / Analyze (L3–L4)**.

---

## 1.5 Block Diagram of a Typical Transmitter System

```
[Process Variable] → [Primary Sensor] → [Signal Conditioner] → [V-to-I Converter] → [4–20 mA Loop] → [Receiver]
   (Level, Flow,        (RTD, Orifice,       (Amplifier,         (Voltage to            (PLC, DCS,
   Temp, Pressure)      Strain Gauge)         Linearizer)         Current Stage)         Recorder)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Static Characteristics of a Transmitter

Every KTU exam question on this experiment tests the student's understanding of the following **static characteristics**. These describe how the transmitter behaves when the input is **held constant** (steady-state) or **changed slowly**.

### 2.1.1 Span

**Definition:** The **algebraic difference** between the maximum and minimum input (or output) values within which the transmitter is designed to operate.

$$\text{Span} = I_{\max} - I_{\min} = O_{\max} - O_{\min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Example: A pressure transmitter with input range 0–10 bar and output 4–20 mA has an **input span of 10 bar** and an **output span of 16 mA**.

### 2.1.2 Zero

**Definition:** The lowest value of the input (or output) at which the transmitter is calibrated to produce the **live-zero** output (4 mA for a current transmitter).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A transmitter calibrated for **0–100 °C** has **Zero = 0 °C** (input) corresponding to **4 mA** (output).

### 2.1.3 Range

**Definition:** The total spread of input values from the **lowest to the highest** (inclusive). For a 0–100 °C transmitter, the range is 0 to 100 °C. **Range = Zero + Span**.

$$\text{Range} = [\text{Zero}, \text{Zero} + \text{Span}]$$

### 2.1.4 Linearity

**Definition:** The closeness with which the actual calibration curve approaches a **straight line** (the ideal/reference line). Usually expressed as **% of Full Scale (FS)** or **% of Span**.

$$\% \text{Linearity} = \frac{\Delta_{\max}^{\text{dev}}}{\text{Span}} \times 100$$

where $\Delta_{\max}^{\text{dev}}$ is the **maximum deviation** of the actual output from the ideal straight line.

> [!TIP]
> **Two Reference Lines Used:**
> 1. **End-Point Line:** Straight line joining zero and full-scale points.
> 2. **Best-Fit Straight Line (Least Squares):** Line that minimizes the sum of squared deviations. KTU typically uses the **end-point line**.

### 2.1.5 Accuracy

**Definition:** The closeness of the **measured value** to the **true value**. It is the **maximum error** between the actual output and the ideal output, expressed as % of full scale.

$$\% \text{Accuracy} = \frac{(O_{\text{actual}} - O_{\text{ideal}})_{\max}}{\text{Full Scale Output}} \times 100$$

### 2.1.6 Hysteresis (or Hysteresis Error)

**Definition:** The **maximum difference** in output readings for the **same input**, taken first with the input **increasing** and then with the input **decreasing**. Caused by friction, backlash, magnetic hysteresis, or thermal lag.

$$\% \text{Hysteresis} = \frac{\Delta H_{\max}}{\text{Span}} \times 100$$

### 2.1.7 Repeatability (Reproducibility)

**Definition:** The ability of the transmitter to **reproduce the same output** for the same input, when the input is applied **repeatedly under identical conditions**, approached from the **same direction**.

$$\% \text{Repeatability} = \frac{(O_{\max} - O_{\min})_{\text{same input, repeated}}}{\text{Span}} \times 100$$

### 2.1.8 Dead Band / Dead Zone

**Definition:** The **smallest range of input values** for which the output **does not change** at all. Below the dead band, the output remains stuck at the live-zero (4 mA).

### 2.1.9 Sensitivity

**Definition:** The **ratio of change in output to the change in input** that produces it. For a linear transmitter:

$$K = \frac{\Delta O}{\Delta I} = \frac{O_{\max} - O_{\min}}{I_{\max} - I_{\min}} = \frac{16 \text{ mA}}{\text{Span}}$$

### 2.1.10 Resolution

**Definition:** The **smallest increment in input** that can be detected and displayed as a change in output. Modern digital transmitters have resolution better than **0.01% of span**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Characteristic | Formula | Unit / % | Key Point |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Span (Input) | $S_{in} = I_{\max} - I_{\min}$ | Same as PV unit | $I_{\min}$ = Zero |
| 2 | Span (Output) | $S_{out} = O_{\max} - O_{\min}$ | mA or V | For 4–20 mA: $S_{out}$ = 16 mA |
| 3 | Range | $R = [\text{Zero}, \text{Zero} + S]$ | PV units | Inclusive of both ends |
| 4 | Sensitivity | $K = \Delta O \div \Delta I$ | mA/unit | Constant for linear TX |
| 5 | % Linearity | $(\Delta_{\max}^{dev} \div S) \times 100$ | % | Use end-point line |
| 6 | % Accuracy | $(\text{Max Error} \div \text{FS}) \times 100$ | % | Includes non-linearity + hysteresis |
| 7 | % Hysteresis | $(\Delta H_{\max} \div S) \times 100$ | % | Up vs down stroke |
| 8 | % Repeatability | $(\Delta R_{\max} \div S) \times 100$ | % | Same direction only |
| 9 | % Dead Band | $(I_{db} \div S) \times 100$ | % | Sub-threshold input range |
| 10 | Output at Input $I$ | $O = 4 + K \cdot (I - I_{\min})$ | mA | Linear interpolation |
| 11 | Input from Output | $I = I_{\min} + \dfrac{(O - 4)}{K}$ | PV unit | Reverse calculation |
| 12 | % of Span | $\dfrac{I - I_{\min}}{S} \times 100$ | % | Used in calibration charts |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly use **% of Full Scale (FS)** where the question asks for **% of Span**. Note that **FS = Span** only when **Zero = 0**. For a transmitter with range **20–100 °C**, FS = 100 °C but Span = 80 °C. This single error can cost **2–3 marks** per question.

---

## 2.3 Transmitter Types by Process Variable

### 2.3.1 Level Transmitters

| Type | Working Principle | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|
| **Differential Pressure (DP)** | Hydrostatic head → DP cell | Open/closed tanks, slurries |
| **Capacitance** | Change in capacitance between probes | Liquids, granular solids, corrosive media |
| **Ultrasonic** | Time-of-flight of ultrasonic pulse | Open tanks, non-contact, dirty liquids |
| **Radar (FMCW/Pulse)** | Microwave time-of-flight | Harsh environments, foam, steam |
| **Float / Displacer** | Buoyancy or float position | Simple point level, clean liquids |
| **Conductivity** | Liquid completes electrode circuit | Conductive liquids only |
| **Hydrostatic (Submersible)** | Pressure at bottom ∝ height | Deep wells, water reservoirs |

### 2.3.2 Flow Transmitters

| Type | Working Principle | Key Equation |
|---|---|---|
| **Differential Pressure (Orifice/Venturi)** | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ | $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$ |
| **Electromagnetic (Magmeter)** | Faraday's law: $E = B \cdot v \cdot D$ | Conductive liquids only |
| **Vortex Shedding** | Frequency of vortex ∝ velocity | $f = \text{St} \cdot v / d$ |
| **Turbine** | Rotor speed ∝ flow rate | Pulses per unit volume |
| **Coriolis** | Mass flow via tube twisting | $F_c = 2 \Delta m \cdot v$ |
| **Ultrasonic** | Doppler shift / time-of-flight | Non-invasive |
| **Thermal Mass** | Heat transfer ∝ mass flow | Gases, low flow |

### 2.3.3 Temperature Transmitters

| Sensor | Working Principle | Range | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **RTD (Pt100)** | Resistance increases linearly with T | –200 to +850 °C | 100 Ω at 0 °C; 0.385 Ω/°C |
| **Thermocouple (K-type)** | Seebeck effect (EMF at junction) | –200 to +1260 °C | ~41 µV/°C |
| **Thermistor** | Resistance decreases (NTC) with T | –55 to +150 °C | Highly non-linear |
| **Bimetallic** | Differential expansion of two metals | –40 to +500 °C | Mechanical deflection |
| **Infrared (Pyrometer)** | Planck's law (black-body radiation) | –50 to +3000 °C | Non-contact |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RTD Callendar-Van Dusen Equation (used in temperature transmitter calibration):**

$$R_T = R_0 \left[ 1 + AT + BT^2 + C(T - 100)T^3 \right]$$

where:
- $R_T$ = resistance at temperature $T$
- $R_0$ = resistance at 0 °C (= 100 Ω for Pt100)
- $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{°C}^{-1}$
- $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{°C}^{-2}$
- $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{°C}^{-4}$ (for $T < 0$ °C, else $C = 0$)

### 2.3.4 Pressure Transmitters

| Type | Working Principle | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Strain Gauge (Bonded/Unbonded)** | Resistance changes with strain on diaphragm | 0–700 bar |
| **Piezoelectric** | Charge generated proportional to force | Dynamic, > 1 kHz response |
| **Capacitive** | Capacitance changes with diaphragm deflection | 0.001–700 bar |
| **Piezo-resistive (Silicon)** | Piezoresistive effect in doped silicon | 0.001–100 bar |
| **Bourdon Tube** | C-shaped tube straightens with pressure | 0.6–7000 bar |
| **Diaphragm** | Flat plate deflection | Low to medium range |

---

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Industry (Real-World Utility)

- **Smart transmitters** (e.g., Rosemount 3051, Yokogawa EJX) use **HART protocol** for digital two-way communication, remote calibration, and self-diagnostics.
- **Accuracy of industrial pressure transmitters**: ±0.04% of span (best in class).
- **4–20 mA current loop advantages**:
  1. **Noise immunity** — current signals are immune to induced voltages from nearby power cables.
  2. **Live-zero fault detection** — distinguishes 0% from wire break.
  3. **Long-distance transmission** — no signal drop over km of cable (since current is the same throughout a series loop).
  4. **Two-wire powering** — many transmitters draw power from the loop itself (loop-powered transmitter).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Universal 4–20 mA Linear Output Equation

### Problem Setup
A transmitter has:
- Input range: $I_{\min}$ to $I_{\max}$ (in process variable units)
- Output range: 4 mA to 20 mA

### Derivation Goal
Derive the equation relating output current $O$ (in mA) to input process variable $I$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Identify the two known input–output points:
- At $I = I_{\min}$: $O = 4 \text{ mA}$
- At $I = I_{\max}$: $O = 20 \text{ mA}$

**Step 2:** The relationship is linear (assumption), so we can use the two-point form:

$$
\frac{O - 4}{I - I_{\min}} = \frac{20 - 4}{I_{\max} - I_{\min}} = \frac{16}{\text{Span}}
$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiplying:

$$
O - 4 = 16 \cdot \frac{I - I_{\min}}{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}
$$

**Step 4:** Adding 4 to both sides yields the **universal linear transmitter equation**:

$$
O = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{I - I_{\min}}{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}
$$

**Step 5 (Sensitivity form):** Define sensitivity $K = 16 / (I_{\max} - I_{\min})$. Then:

$$
O = 4 + K (I - I_{\min}) \quad \text{[mA]}
$$

**Step 6 (Reverse — input from output):** Solving for $I$:

$$
I = I_{\min} + \frac{O - 4}{K} = I_{\min} + \frac{O - 4}{16} \cdot (I_{\max} - I_{\min})
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Application Tip:** Memorize Step 4's formula. It is used in **at least 3 out of 10** previous-year transmitter problems. Substituting the given values into this single equation solves ~80% of calibration questions.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 1 — Temperature Transmitter

**Question (Typical KTU 2-mark):** A Pt100 RTD is connected to a temperature transmitter calibrated for a range of **0–200 °C** with a **4–20 mA** output. Find the output current when the measured temperature is **125 °C**.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Identify parameters:
- $I_{\min} = 0 \; \text{°C}$
- $I_{\max} = 200 \; \text{°C}$
- $O_{\min} = 4 \text{ mA}$, $O_{\max} = 20 \text{ mA}$
- $I = 125 \; \text{°C}$

**Step 2:** Apply the universal equation:

$$
O = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{I - I_{\min}}{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}
$$

$$
O = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{125 - 0}{200 - 0}
$$

$$
O = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{125}{200}
$$

$$
O = 4 + 16 \cdot 0.625
$$

$$
O = 4 + 10.0 = 14.0 \text{ mA}
$$

**Result:** The transmitter output is **14 mA**, corresponding to 62.5% of span (which is correct: 125/200 = 62.5%).

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 2 — Pressure Transmitter with Hysteresis

**Question:** A pressure transmitter is calibrated for **0–10 bar** with **4–20 mA** output. During testing, the following readings were taken:

| Input (bar) | Output Increasing (mA) | Output Decreasing (mA) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 4.05 | 4.10 |
| 2 | 7.10 | 7.18 |
| 4 | 10.20 | 10.25 |
| 6 | 13.15 | 13.22 |
| 8 | 16.05 | 16.10 |
| 10 | 20.00 | 20.05 |

Calculate the **% Hysteresis** (with respect to span).

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the **hysteresis error** at each input point (decreasing − increasing):

$$
\Delta H_i = O_{\text{down}, i} - O_{\text{up}, i}
$$

- At 0 bar: $4.10 - 4.05 = 0.05 \text{ mA}$
- At 2 bar: $7.18 - 7.10 = 0.08 \text{ mA}$
- At 4 bar: $10.25 - 10.20 = 0.05 \text{ mA}$
- At 6 bar: $13.22 - 13.15 = 0.07 \text{ mA}$
- At 8 bar: $16.10 - 16.05 = 0.05 \text{ mA}$
- At 10 bar: $20.05 - 20.00 = 0.05 \text{ mA}$

**Step 2:** Identify the **maximum** hysteresis error:

$$
\Delta H_{\max} = 0.08 \text{ mA} \quad \text{(at 2 bar)}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the output span:

$$
S_{out} = 20 - 4 = 16 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 4:** Apply the hysteresis formula:

$$
\% \text{ Hysteresis} = \frac{\Delta H_{\max}}{S_{out}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Hysteresis} = \frac{0.08}{16} \times 100 = 0.5\%
$$

**Result:** The transmitter exhibits a hysteresis of **0.5% of span**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem 3 — Level Transmitter with Linearity

**Question:** A capacitive level transmitter is calibrated for **0–5 m water level** with **4–20 mA** output. Test data:

| Level (m) | Ideal Output (mA) | Actual Output (mA) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 4.00 | 4.00 |
| 1.0 | 7.20 | 7.30 |
| 2.0 | 10.40 | 10.50 |
| 3.0 | 13.60 | 13.50 |
| 4.0 | 16.80 | 16.90 |
| 5.0 | 20.00 | 20.00 |

Calculate **% Non-Linearity** (using end-point method).

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the deviation at each point:

$$
\Delta_i = O_{\text{actual}, i} - O_{\text{ideal}, i}
$$

- At 1.0 m: $7.30 - 7.20 = +0.10 \text{ mA}$
- At 2.0 m: $10.50 - 10.40 = +0.10 \text{ mA}$
- At 3.0 m: $13.50 - 13.60 = -0.10 \text{ mA}$
- At 4.0 m: $16.90 - 16.80 = +0.10 \text{ mA}$

**Step 2:** The maximum deviation magnitude:

$$
\vert \Delta \vert_{\max} = 0.10 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 3:** Span = 16 mA. Apply formula:

$$
\% \text{ Linearity} = \frac{\vert \Delta \vert_{\max}}{\text{Span}} \times 100 = \frac{0.10}{16} \times 100 = 0.625\%
$$

**Result:** Linearity error is **±0.625% of span**.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem 4 — Flow Transmitter (Orifice + DP Cell)

**Question:** An orifice plate flow transmitter measures water flow. The DP cell range is **0–2500 mm WC** (water column). The flow range is **0–50 m³/h**. Determine the flow rate when the DP cell output is **12 mA** (live zero = 4 mA, span = 16 mA).

### Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the % of span corresponding to 12 mA:

$$
\% \text{ Span} = \frac{12 - 4}{20 - 4} \times 100 = \frac{8}{16} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

**Step 2:** Since flow $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$, the flow at 50% DP is:

$$
Q = Q_{\max} \cdot \sqrt{0.50} = 50 \cdot \sqrt{0.5} = 50 \cdot 0.7071 = 35.36 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Result:** The flow rate is **≈ 35.36 m³/h**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Distinction:** Flow is **NOT linear** with DP. A flow transmitter with **linearized output** is required to provide a linear 4–20 mA signal corresponding to actual flow. Otherwise, the relationship is square-root. This is a **5-mark KTU favourite**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference for Lab Calculations

| Characteristic | What to Calculate | Symbol Used | Numerator | Denominator | Final Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Span | $I_{\max} - I_{\min}$ | $S$ | Difference of inputs | — | PV unit |
| Sensitivity | $\Delta O / \Delta I$ | $K$ | Output change | Input change | mA/unit |
| % Linearity | Max deviation / Span | $L$ | $\vert \Delta \vert_{\max}$ | Span | % |
| % Hysteresis | Max up-down difference | $H$ | $\Delta H_{\max}$ | Span | % |
| % Repeatability | Max spread in repeats | $R$ | $\Delta R_{\max}$ | Span | % |
| % Dead Band | Threshold / Span | $DB$ | $I_{db}$ | Span | % |
| % Accuracy | Total max error / FS | $A$ | $(O_a - O_i)_{\max}$ | Full scale | % |

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Generic Process Transmitter

```mermaid
graph LR
    PV[Process Variable<br>Level, Flow, Temp, Pressure]
    SEN[Primary Sensor<br>RTD, Orifice, Strain Gauge, Diaphragm]
    SIGCON[Signal Conditioner<br>Amplifier, Linearizer, Cold Junction Comp]
    VIC[Voltage to Current<br>Converter Stage]
    LOOP[4 to 20 mA<br>Current Loop Output]
    POW[Loop Power Supply<br>24 V DC]
    RX[Receiver<br>PLC, DCS, Indicator, Recorder]
    DISP[Display<br>Engineering Units]
    CAL[Calibration Resistor<br>250 Ohm Shunt for V out]

    PV --> SEN
    SEN --> SIGCON
    SIGCON --> VIC
    VIC --> LOOP
    POW --> VIC
    LOOP --> RX
    LOOP --> CAL
    CAL --> DISP
```

---

## 4.2 Transmitter Calibration Test Bench Setup

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph INPUT[Input Calibration Source]
        DC[Dead Weight Tester<br>Pressure Input]
        BF[Burette + Scale<br>Level Input]
        FB[Flow Calibration Rig<br>Rotameter Standard]
        DR[Dry Block Calibrator<br>Temperature Input]
    end

    subgraph TRANSMITTER[Device Under Test]
        TX[Industrial Transmitter<br>4 to 20 mA Output]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Measurement]
        MA[Precision mA Meter<br>0.001 mA resolution]
        V[Voltmeter across 250 Ohm<br>1 to 5 V display]
        REC[Strip Chart Recorder<br>or PC DAQ]
    end

    DC --> TX
    BF --> TX
    FB --> TX
    DR --> TX
    TX --> MA
    TX --> V
    TX --> REC
```

---

## 4.3 Signal Flow for a 4-Wire vs 2-Wire Transmitter

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TW[2 Wire Loop Powered Transmitter]
        PS2[24 V DC Supply]
        R2[250 Ohm Resistor]
        TX2[Transmitter]
        PS2 --> R2
        R2 --> TX2
        TX2 --> PS2
    end

    subgraph FW[4 Wire Active Transmitter]
        PS4[24 V DC Supply to TX]
        TX4[Transmitter Internal Power]
        OUT4[4 to 20 mA Output]

        PS4 --> TX4
        TX4 --> OUT4
        OUT4 --> RL4
    end
```

---

```mermaid

    A[Choose Transmitter Type] --> B{Process Variable}
    B -->|Level| C{Contact Allowed}

```

## 4.5 Calibration Curve Schematic (Input vs Output)

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Input Process Variable Percent of Span 0 to 100] --> Y[Output mA 4 to 20]
    X1[0 percent] --> Y1[4 mA Ideal]
    X2[25 percent] --> Y2[8 mA Ideal]
    X3[50 percent] --> Y3[12 mA Ideal]
    X4[75 percent] --> Y4[16 mA Ideal]
    X5[100 percent] --> Y5[20 mA Ideal]

    X2 -.Actual.-> Y2A[7.95 mA Deviation minus 0.05]
    X4 -.Actual.-> Y4A[16.08 mA Deviation plus 0.08]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The deviation $\Delta$ at any input is the vertical distance between the **ideal straight line** and the **actual calibration curve**. The maximum such $\Delta$ is used in the **% Linearity** calculation.
