| **Exposure** | Safe to embed in HTML/JS | **Must remain secret** (server-side only) |

**Security reason:** If the Write API Key is exposed, any malicious user on the internet can inject **false sensor data** into the channel, corrupt analytics, or exhaust the daily message quota. A leaked key must be immediately regenerated from the ThingSpeak dashboard to prevent abuse.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of uploading temperature and humidity data from a DHT11 sensor to the ThingSpeak cloud using a NodeMCU ESP8266. Clearly label all the five layers of the IoT reference model. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the complete circuit diagram for interfacing a DHT11 with NodeMCU. Write the Arduino code to read temperature and humidity and upload to ThingSpeak every 20 seconds. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
The five layers of the IoT architecture are:
1. **Perception Layer:** DHT11 sensor — a single-wire digital sensor with an on-chip NTC thermistor and capacitive humidity element. Output is a 40-bit serial frame every 2 seconds.
2. **Network Layer:** NodeMCU's integrated **IEEE 802.11 b/g/n Wi-Fi transceiver** establishes a TCP connection to ThingSpeak's public IP.
3. **Middleware / Cloud Layer:** ThingSpeak's **REST API endpoint** `api.thingspeak.com/update` receives the data and writes it to a time-series database.
4. **Application Layer:** The **ThingSpeak web dashboard** renders Field 1 and Field 2 as line charts, gauges, and numeric displays.
5. **Business Layer:** End-users (farmers, lab supervisors) view the data on browsers or mobile apps and trigger **React Apps** for alerts.

(Block diagram as in §4.1.)

#### Model Solution for (b):
**Circuit connections** (table as in §3.2). The complete Arduino sketch as in §3.3 should be reproduced with the following key points called out:
- `#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>` for Wi-Fi driver
- `#include <DHT.h>` for the sensor library
- `WiFi.begin(ssid, password)` to associate with the access point
- `HTTPClient http; http.begin(url); http.GET();` to issue the upload
- `millis()`-based non-blocking scheduler to enforce the 20-second interval

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Explain the 40-bit data frame format of a DHT11 sensor. How is checksum validation performed? Why is checksum validation critical for reliable cloud uploads? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A green-house monitoring system uploads one temperature and one humidity value every 15 seconds to ThingSpeak. Calculate (i) the number of entries uploaded per hour, (ii) per day, and (iii) the percentage of the free-tier daily quota (8,200 entries) that is consumed per day. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):
The 40-bit frame is structured as:
- Byte 1 (8 bits): Temperature integer part (°C)
- Byte 2 (8 bits): Temperature decimal part (always 0 for DHT11)
- Byte 3 (8 bits): Humidity integer part (%)
- Byte 4 (8 bits): Humidity decimal part (always 0 for DHT11)
- Byte 5 (8 bits): Checksum = lower 8 bits of (B1 + B2 + B3 + B4)

**Checksum equation:**

$$C = (B_1 + B_2 + B_3 + B_4) \bmod 256$$

If the received checksum ≠ computed checksum, the entire packet is **discarded** and a fresh read is initiated. This validation is critical for cloud uploads because:
1. **Garbage-in-garbage-out prevention** — A corrupted value uploaded to the cloud can trigger false alerts (e.g., spurious "fire alarm" in a cold-chain monitor).
2. **Cost of data integrity** — Cloud storage and analytics cost is paid for every entry; uploading invalid data wastes quota.
3. **Trigger reliability** — ThingSpeak React triggers depend on accurate field values; corrupted data causes misfires.

#### Model Solution for (b):
**Given:** Update interval $T = 15$ seconds.

**(i) Entries per hour:**

$$N_{hour} = \frac{3600}{T} = \frac{3600}{15} = 240 \text{ entries/hour}$$

**(ii) Entries per day:**

$$N_{day} = \frac{86400}{T} = \frac{86400}{15} = 5760 \text{ entries/day}$$

**(iii) Percentage of free-tier quota consumed:**

$$P = \frac{N_{day}}{\text{Quota}} \times 100 = \frac{5760}{8200} \times 100 = 70.24\%$$

The system therefore consumes **70.24 %** of the free-tier daily message budget, leaving a safety margin of 2,440 entries for retries, manual uploads, or temporary data bursts.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Read Before Submitting Lab Record):**
> 1. **Wrong baud rate** — Using `Serial.begin(9600)` instead of `115200` produces garbage in the Serial Monitor. Examiners may deduct 0.5 mark for this.
> 2. **Missing delay between uploads** — Uploading every 2 seconds will return HTTP code `400` from ThingSpeak. Always use a `>= 15 s` interval.
> 3. **Forgetting the pull-up resistor on the DHT data line** — Causes intermittent `NaN` readings and frustrated debugging sessions.
> 4. **Exposing the Write API Key in the lab record** — Examiners may treat this as a security violation. Either mask it as `K9P3****WX1Y` or use a placeholder.
> 5. **Drawing a block diagram of only the DHT11 internal circuit** — Examiners expect the **system-level** IoT diagram, not the sensor schematic.
> 6. **Forgetting to verify checksum** — Always use `isnan()` guard before HTTP upload to prevent corrupt data from being published.
> 7. **Hard-coding the Wi-Fi credentials in plain text** — Acceptable for lab use, but examiners appreciate seeing a comment recommending the use of `WiFiManager` for production.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before Exam**
> - **ThingSpeak** is a MathWorks IoT cloud platform using a RESTful HTTP API.
> - A **Channel** has 8 Fields, 1 status field, and a location field.
> - **Write API Key** is private; **Read API Key** is shareable.
> - **Minimum update interval** is **15 seconds** (free tier).
> - The **DHT11** outputs a **40-bit frame** with a **checksum** as the last 8 bits.
> - **Checksum = (B1 + B2 + B3 + B4) & 0xFF**. If mismatched, discard the packet.
> - **NodeMCU** uses **3.3 V logic**; never apply 5 V to its GPIO pins.
> - The **HTTP request** is a `GET /update?api_key=...&field1=...&field2=...` to `api.thingspeak.com`.
> - A successful upload returns **HTTP 200** and the body contains the new **Entry ID**.
> - Always use a **non-blocking scheduler** (`millis()`) instead of `delay()`.
> - **Free-tier daily quota** = 8,200 entries; **daily cap** = `86400 / T_interval`.
> - The **Rothfusz Heat Index** formula (Steadman 1979) gives the "feels-like" temperature and is commonly used in green-house and HVAC applications.
> - The **Dew Point** (Magnus-Tetens formula) is important for **condensation control** in industrial cold-chain storage.
> - **Wi-Fi RSSI** in dBm is the primary metric for diagnosing link quality.
> - **Critical security practice:** Treat the Write API Key as a password; regenerate immediately if leaked.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Retrieval of temperature and humidity data from thingspeak cloud
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCICL508 - INDUSTRIAL INSTRUMENTATION & IOT LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Retrieval of temperature and humidity data from thingspeak cloud

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ThingSpeak** is an Internet of Things (IoT) analytics platform hosted on the MathWorks cloud infrastructure that allows devices to **publish**, **retrieve**, and **analyze** live sensor data streams via standard **HTTP/HTTPS** RESTful API calls. In the context of this experiment, it serves as a **persistent cloud data store** for temperature and humidity telemetry, accessible globally for monitoring, visualization, and downstream analytics.

The experiment demonstrates the **Read API (GET request)** pathway of ThingSpeak, where a client (microcontroller, PC, or mobile) pulls the most recent or historical temperature (`field1`) and humidity (`field2`) values from a configured channel using a **Read API Key** and processes the returned **JSON payload**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Letterbox" Model

Imagine ThingSpeak as a **central digital letterbox** maintained by the post office:

1. Your **DHT11/DHT22 sensor** is a person who writes two numbers on a postcard every minute: *temperature* and *humidity*.
2. The **microcontroller (ESP8266/ESP32)** is the postman who walks to the letterbox and drops the postcard inside (the *Write* operation).
3. The **ThingSpeak cloud** stores every postcard in a numbered, time-stamped slot (a *feed*).
4. When you want to know the values, you call a *different* postman (your **HTTP GET request**) and say, *"Please bring me the last 20 postcards from channel 234567"*. That postman returns with the postcards, and you read the numbers — this entire trip is the **data retrieval** process.

The "address" you use to call the retrieval postman is built from three ingredients: the **Channel ID**, the **Read API Key**, and an **optional query count**.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> The following standard metrics and constants govern this experiment:
>
> - **HTTP Status Code 200** → indicates a successful GET retrieval
> - **HTTP Status Code 404** → invalid channel ID or API key
> - **Relative Humidity (RH)** is measured in **%RH** (range: **0–100 %**)
> - **Temperature** is typically stored in **°C** (DHT11 range: **0–50 °C**, DHT22 range: **−40 to 80 °C**)
> - **JSON** (JavaScript Object Notation) is the universal data-interchange format used by ThingSpeak
> - **Sample window**: ThingSpeak free tier enforces a **minimum 15-second** update interval per channel
> - **UTC timestamp** (Coordinated Universal Time) is used for all feed entries

## 1.4 Aim of the Experiment

To interface a **DHT11/DHT22** temperature–humidity sensor with an **ESP8266/ESP32** microcontroller, push the readings to a ThingSpeak channel, and **retrieve** the stored data using an HTTP GET request (either directly from the board or from a separate client such as a Python/Processing/Node-RED dashboard).

## 1.5 Apparatus and Software Stack

| Component | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| ESP8266 NodeMCU / ESP32 DevKit | Wi-Fi enabled microcontroller acting as IoT node |
| DHT11 or DHT22 sensor | Combined temperature & humidity transducer |
| 10 kΩ pull-up resistor | Required on DHT data line (some modules have it onboard) |
| Breadboard & jumper wires | Circuit assembly |
| ThingSpeak account (free tier) | Cloud channel & API key provisioning |
| Arduino IDE 1.8.x / 2.x | Firmware development environment |
| Libraries: `ESP8266WiFi.h`, `DHT.h`, `ThingSpeak.h` | Network, sensor, and cloud abstraction |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Flow (Why and How)

The retrieval operation is governed by the **client–server REST model**. Each bullet explains the *Why* and the *How*:

- **Why is HTTP used?** Because it is the universal, firewall-friendly, text-based protocol that every Wi-Fi module can speak.
  *How:* The ESP module opens a TCP socket on port **80** (HTTP) or **443** (HTTPS) and sends a `GET` request line.

- **Why is an API key needed?** The key is the **authentication token** that proves the caller is authorized to read the private channel.
  *How:* It is appended as a query parameter `api_key=XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX` in the URL.

- **Why JSON?** JSON is **lightweight**, **human-readable**, and **natively understood** by JavaScript, Python, and most modern parsers.
  *How:* ThingSpeak serializes each feed entry as a `{ "created_at": "...", "field1": "..", "field2": ".." }` object.

- **Why a separate "Read" and "Write" key?** This implements **Principle of Least Privilege** — a monitoring dashboard can read data without being able to overwrite sensor values.

- **Why are `field1` and `field2` used?** ThingSpeak allows up to **8 numeric fields** per channel; here `field1 = temperature` and `field2 = humidity` by convention.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas and conversions relevant to the **retrieval, interpretation, and validation** of temperature/humidity data from ThingSpeak are summarized below.

| Quantity / Relation | Formula / Expression | Units | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Celsius → Fahrenheit | $T_{F} = T_{C} \times \dfrac{9}{5} + 32$ | °F | Display for non-metric dashboards |
| Fahrenheit → Celsius | $T_{C} = (T_{F} - 32) \times \dfrac{5}{9}$ | °C | Sensor calibration cross-check |
| Relative Humidity ratio | $RH_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{RH_{\text{measured}}}{100}$ | dimensionless | Used in dew-point calculation |
| Dew Point (Magnus form) | $T_{d} = \dfrac{b \cdot \gamma(T,RH)}{a - \gamma(T,RH)}$ where $\gamma = \ln(RH/100) + \dfrac{aT}{b+T}$ with $a=17.27$, $b=237.7$ | °C | HVAC / cold-chain analytics |
| Heat Index (Rothfusz) | $HI = -42.379 + 2.04901523\,T + 10.14333127\,RH - 0.22475541\,T\cdot RH - 0.00683783\,T^2 - 0.05481717\,RH^2 + 0.00122874\,T^2\cdot RH + 0.00085282\,T\cdot RH^2 - 0.00000199\,T^2\cdot RH^2$ | °F | "Feels like" temperature |
| HTTP URL (Read feeds) | `https://api.thingspeak.com/channels/<id>/feeds.json?api_key=<key>&results=<n>` | URL | Retrieval of last *n* records |
| HTTP URL (Read last entry) | `https://api.thingspeak.com/channels/<id>/feeds/last.json?api_key=<key>` | URL | Pulls the single most recent feed |
| HTTP URL (Read by date) | `https://api.thingspeak.com/channels/<id>/feeds.json?api_key=<key>&start=YYYY-MM-DD&end=YYYY-MM-DD` | URL | Historical window queries |
| Sampling period (free tier) | $T_{s} \ge 15\,\text{s}$ | seconds | Rate-limit compliance |
| JSON field count | Up to **8 fields** per channel | — | Channel capacity limit |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Cloud retrieval of environmental telemetry is the cornerstone of:
> - **Smart Agriculture** — soil and ambient microclimate monitoring in polyhouses
> - **Pharmaceutical Cold Chain** — vaccine storage compliance (WHO PQS E006)
> - **Data Center Cooling** — rack inlet temperature surveillance
> - **Industrial HVAC** — multi-zone comfort control
> - **Weather Station Networks** — citizen-science mesh deployments

The specific ability to **retrieve** (not merely write) data is what makes the system *useful* — it enables dashboards, ML pipelines, threshold alerts, and historical analytics that operate **independently** of the sensor node.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Parsing Logic & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the ThingSpeak GET Request URL

A valid retrieval URL is constructed by concatenating the **base endpoint**, **channel ID**, **sub-resource**, **API key**, and **query parameters**. We derive each component below.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Base endpoint (HTTPS)} &\;=\; \text{https://api.thingspeak.com} \\
\text{Channel resource} &\;=\; \text{/channels/} \oplus \text{ChannelID} \\
\text{Sub-resource} &\;\in\; \{\text{/feeds.json},\ \text{/feeds/last.json},\ \text{/fields/}\langle f\rangle\text{.json}\} \\
\text{Auth token} &\;=\; \text{?api_key=} \oplus \text{ReadAPIKey} \\
\text{Optional filters} &\;=\; \text{\&results=}\langle n\rangle \;\vert\; \text{\&start=}\langle d\rangle \;\vert\; \text{\&end=}\langle d\rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Concatenate base + channel + sub-resource.** This identifies *which* channel and *what* portion of its data is requested. For a single latest reading:

$$
\text{URL}_{1} = \text{https://api.thingspeak.com/channels/}1234567\text{/feeds/last.json}
$$

**Step 2 — Append the Read API key as a query parameter.** Without the key, ThingSpeak returns **HTTP 401/404** (unauthorized) for a private channel:

$$
\text{URL}_{2} = \text{URL}_{1} \oplus \text{?api_key=} \oplus \text{ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP}
$$

**Step 3 — Append the result count to retrieve multiple historical points.** A count of `n=20` returns the last 20 feeds:

$$
\text{URL}_{3} = \text{URL}_{2} \oplus \text{\&results=}20
$$

**Final consolidated retrieval URL** (example with ChannelID = 1234567, ReadKey = ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP, n = 20):

$$
\boxed{\text{URL}_{\text{final}} = \text{https://api.thingspeak.com/channels/1234567/feeds.json?api_key=ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP&results=20}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The symbols $\oplus$ denote string concatenation, and the vertical bar $\vert$ (typed as `\vert`) above separates mutually exclusive optional parameters. **Never** use the bare `|` character inside a markdown table — it breaks the table parser.

## 3.2 Step-by-Step JSON Response Decomposition

A typical JSON response from ThingSpeak for two-field temperature/humidity is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Response} = \{\, &\text{"channel"}:\{ \text{"id"}:1234567,\ \text{"name"}:\text{"EnvLab}"\}, \\
&\text{"feeds"}:[ \\
&\;\;\{\text{"created_at"}:\text{"2024-09-15T10:00:12Z"},\ \text{"field1"}:\text{"28.40"},\ \text{"field2"}:\text{"62.10"}\}, \\
&\;\;\{\text{"created_at"}:\text{"2024-09-15T10:01:14Z"},\ \text{"field1"}:\text{"28.60"},\ \text{"field2"}:\text{"61.80"}\} \\
&] \\
&\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Locate the `feeds` array.** The wrapper object has two children: `channel` (metadata) and `feeds` (the actual time-series). Parsing must descend into `feeds[0]`, `feeds[1]`, …

**Step 2 — Extract `field1` (temperature) and `field2` (humidity) per element.** Note that **all field values are returned as strings** by the ThingSpeak API — they must be cast to `float` (in C/C++) or `float()` (in Python) before arithmetic.

**Step 3 — Convert string to numeric.** In Arduino C++:

$$
T_{C} = \text{StringToFloat}(\text{JSON}[\text{"field1"}])
$$

**Step 4 — Optionally apply unit conversion** (e.g., to Fahrenheit):

$$
T_{F} = T_{C} \times 1.8 + 32
$$

**Step 5 — Validate bounds.** Reject any reading outside $[-40, 80]$ °C for a DHT22 (sanity check):

$$
\text{Valid}(T_C) = \begin{cases} \text{true}, & -40 \le T_C \le 80 \\ \text{false}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

## 3.3 Reference Arduino Sketch (ESP8266/ESP32 — Read from ThingSpeak)

```cpp
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>          // (use <WiFi.h> for ESP32)
#include <ThingSpeak.h>

const char* ssid      = "YOUR_WIFI_SSID";
const char* password  = "YOUR_WIFI_PASS";

unsigned long channelID   = 1234567;
const char*   readAPIKey  = "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP";

WiFiClient client;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  WiFi.begin(ssid, password);
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) { delay(500); Serial.print("."); }
  ThingSpeak.begin(client);
}

void loop() {
  float temperature = ThingSpeak.readFloatField(channelID, 1, readAPIKey);
  float humidity    = ThingSpeak.readFloatField(channelID, 2, readAPIKey);

  if (temperature == -999.0 || humidity == -999.0) {
    Serial.println("Error: invalid read (rate limit or bad key).");
  } else {
    Serial.print("Temp: "); Serial.print(temperature); Serial.println(" C");
    Serial.print("RH  : "); Serial.print(humidity);    Serial.println(" %");
  }
  delay(20000);   // respect the 15 s ThingSpeak free-tier window
}
```

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Reference Python Snippet (Dashboard / Laptop Retrieval)

```python
import urllib.request, json

CHANNEL_ID  = "1234567"
READ_KEY    = "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOP"
URL = f"https://api.thingspeak.com/channels/{CHANNEL_ID}/feeds/last.json?api_key={READ_KEY}"

with urllib.request.urlopen(URL) as response:
    payload = json.loads(response.read().decode())

temperature_c = float(payload["field1"])
humidity_pct  = float(payload["field2"])
print(f"Temp = {temperature_c:.2f} C   |   RH = {humidity_pct:.2f} %")
```

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Pitfalls in Retrieval

| Pitfall | Symptom | Root Cause | Mitigation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `-999.0` returned | Library sentinel value | Bad API key or rate-limit breach | Verify key, add `delay(20000)` |
| `field1` is empty string | `StringToFloat` returns `NaN` | Sensor not yet uploaded | Filter empty strings before casting |
| HTTP 401/404 | Connection refused | Wrong Channel ID / private channel w/o key | Double-check channel privacy toggle |
| Stale timestamp | `created_at` older than expected | Node offline / Wi-Fi lost | Add watchdog timer in node firmware |
| Offset timezone | Display misaligned with local time | UTC returned by default | Apply `+05:30` for IST manually |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End System Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[DHT11 or DHT22 Sensor] --> B[ESP8266 or ESP32 Microcontroller]
    B -->|HTTP POST field1 and field2| C[ThingSpeak Cloud Channel]
    C -->|Persistent storage as JSON feeds| D[ThingSpeak Database]
    D -->|HTTP GET request with read API key| E[Client Dashboard or Retrieval App]
    E -->|Parse JSON and convert units| F[Display Temperature in C and Humidity in percent]
    C -->|Built-in widgets| G[ThingSpeak Web Visualizations]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: The left-to-right arrows trace a *telemetry pipeline*. The top route (A → B → C → D → E → F) is the **path of the data value**; the bottom branch (C → G) is the **path of the visualization** rendered by ThingSpeak's native MATLAB-powered charts.

## 4.2 Retrieval State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> WiFiConnect
    WiFiConnect --> AuthReady : SSID and PASS valid
    WiFiConnect --> Retry : Connection failed
    Retry --> WiFiConnect : after 5 s
    AuthReady --> SendGET : Build URL with channelID and readAPIKey
    SendGET --> WaitResponse : HTTP GET to api.thingspeak.com
    WaitResponse --> ParseJSON : status 200
    WaitResponse --> HandleError : status 4xx or 5xx
    ParseJSON --> Display : cast field1 and field2 to float
    HandleError --> AuthReady : log and back off for 20 s
    Display --> Sleep : delay 20 s
    Sleep --> SendGET : loop iteration
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Mapping (Block-Level)

| Stage | Hardware / Software Module | Input | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sensing | DHT11 / DHT22 | Ambient air | Digital pulse containing T and RH |
| Acquisition | ESP8266 / ESP32 firmware | Single-wire digital protocol | Float `T` and `RH` |
| Transmission (Write) | `ThingSpeak.writeFields()` | `T`, `RH` | HTTP POST packet |
| Storage | ThingSpeak cloud | JSON over HTTPS | Time-stamped `feed` record |
| Retrieval (Read) | `ThingSpeak.readFloatField()` or Python `urllib` | Channel ID + read API key | JSON response |
| Presentation | Serial Monitor / Matplotlib / Mobile App | Float values | Human-readable chart |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Differentiate between the Read API Key and the Write API Key of a ThingSpeak channel. Why are they kept separate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) The **Write API Key** authorizes a device to *upload* new feed entries to a specific channel field.
- (1 mark) The **Read API Key** authorizes a client application to *download* the stored feed data.
- (1 mark) They are kept separate to implement the **Principle of Least Privilege** — a monitoring dashboard can retrieve data without being able to inject or overwrite sensor values, thereby preventing spoofing and unauthorized tampering.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Sample]`
**What is the minimum update interval allowed on the ThingSpeak free-tier plan, and what HTTP status code is returned on a successful GET request?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1.5 marks) The minimum update interval on the free tier is **15 seconds**. Posting more frequently results in HTTP **400 Bad Request** with a `"-Rate limit exceeded"` response.
- (1.5 marks) A successful GET request returns HTTP status code **200 OK**, with the feed data serialized as a JSON payload.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 2]`**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a ThingSpeak-based IoT system for monitoring ambient temperature and humidity. Label all blocks and data directions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Write the complete Arduino sketch to **retrieve** the most recent temperature and humidity values from a ThingSpeak channel with Channel ID `1872043` and Read API Key `XYZ123ABC456DEF7` using an ESP8266, and display them on the Serial Monitor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **(1.5 marks)** Draw a six-block diagram: *DHT Sensor → ESP8266 → Wi-Fi Router → ThingSpeak Cloud → Database → Retrieval Client (PC/Mobile)*, with arrows indicating bidirectional read/write flows.
2. **(1.5 marks)** State that the DHT11/DHT22 uses a single-wire digital protocol (1 mark) and that the ESP8266 uses the IEEE 802.11 b/g/n 2.4 GHz radio (0.5 mark).
3. **(1.5 marks)** Explain that the cloud stores each `feed` as `{created_at, field1, field2, ...}` (1 mark), and that the channel can be configured as *Public* or *Private* with corresponding API key gating (0.5 mark).
4. **(1 mark)** Mention that ThingSpeak supports MATLAB analytics — running moving-average, threshold-alert, or FFT scripts server-side.
5. **(1 mark)** Note that the **Read API** path uses HTTP **GET** and the **Write API** path uses HTTP **POST** (or GET with `update?api_key=...&field1=...` for legacy code).
6. **(0.5 mark)** Conclude with a one-line statement of application (e.g., "Used in smart-greenhouse climate control").

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

```cpp
#include <ESP8266WiFi.h>
#include <ThingSpeak.h>

const char* ssid     = "LabWiFi";
const char* password = "lab12345";
unsigned long channelID  = 1872043;
const char*  readKey    = "XYZ123ABC456DEF7";
WiFiClient client;

void setup() {
  Serial.begin(115200);
  WiFi.begin(ssid, password);
  while (WiFi.status() != WL_CONNECTED) { delay(500); Serial.print("."); }
  ThingSpeak.begin(client);
}

void loop() {
  float T = ThingSpeak.readFloatField(channelID, 1, readKey);
  float H = ThingSpeak.readFloatField(channelID, 2, readKey);
  if (T == -999.0 || H == -999.0) {
    Serial.println("Read error: check key / rate limit.");
  } else {
    Serial.print("Temperature: "); Serial.print(T); Serial.println(" C");
    Serial.print("Humidity   : "); Serial.print(H); Serial.println(" %");
  }
  delay(20000);
}
```

**Mark split for (b):**
- (1 mark) Correct library inclusion.
- (1 mark) Valid Wi-Fi credentials and Wi-Fi connect routine.
- (1 mark) Correct `ThingSpeak.begin()` call.
- (2 marks) Correct use of `readFloatField` with proper arguments (channelID, field number, key).
- (1 mark) Sentinel-value error check for `-999.0`.
- (1 mark) Serial print formatting and `delay(20000)` for free-tier compliance.

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 2 Alt Choice]`**
>
> **(a)** Explain the JSON structure returned by the ThingSpeak Read API when querying the *last 5 feeds* of a temperature–humidity channel. Sketch a representative response and identify the three mandatory fields. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A DHT22 sensor reports a temperature of **28.6 °C** and a relative humidity of **62 %**. Compute (i) the equivalent Fahrenheit temperature, and (ii) the dew point using the Magnus formula. Show every algebraic step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **(2 marks)** Sketch the JSON tree: root `{` → child `channel{...}`, child `feeds[ ... ]`, where each feed is `{created_at, field1, field2}`.
2. **(1 mark)** Identify the three mandatory fields per feed: `created_at` (UTC timestamp), `field1` (temperature as string), `field2` (humidity as string).
3. **(1 mark)** Note that the wrapper also contains `channel_id`, `name`, `description`, and `latitude`/`longitude` metadata.
4. **(1 mark)** Explain that all numeric fields are returned as **quoted strings** to preserve precision and require explicit `float()` casting.
5. **(1 mark)** Discuss array indexing: `feeds[0]` is the most recent, `feeds[n-1]` is the oldest of the *n* retrieved.
6. **(1 mark)** Mention HTTP response headers — `Content-Type: application/json` and `ETag` for caching.

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Fahrenheit conversion (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{F} &= T_{C} \times \frac{9}{5} + 32 \\
T_{F} &= 28.6 \times \frac{9}{5} + 32 \\
T_{F} &= 28.6 \times 1.8 + 32 \\
T_{F} &= 51.48 + 32 \\
\boxed{T_{F} &= 83.48\;^{\circ}\text{F}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Dew point via Magnus formula (5 marks):**

The Magnus form is:

$$
T_{d} = \frac{b \cdot \gamma(T,RH)}{a - \gamma(T,RH)} \quad \text{where} \quad \gamma = \ln\!\left(\frac{RH}{100}\right) + \frac{aT}{b+T}
$$

with $a = 17.27$ and $b = 237.7$ (°C).

**Step 1 — Compute the $\ln$ term:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{62}{100}\right) = \ln(0.62) = -0.4780
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the $\dfrac{aT}{b+T}$ term:**

$$
\frac{aT}{b+T} = \frac{17.27 \times 28.6}{237.7 + 28.6} = \frac{493.922}{266.3} = 1.8547
$$

**Step 3 — Add to get $\gamma$:**

$$
\gamma = -0.4780 + 1.8547 = 1.3767
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the dew-point equation:**

$$
T_{d} = \frac{237.7 \times 1.3767}{17.27 - 1.3767} = \frac{327.2415}{15.8933} = 20.59\;^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{d} \approx 20.6\;^{\circ}\text{C}}
$$

**Interpretation (implicit, 0 mark but expected in viva):** Condensation forms if a surface is cooled to ~20.6 °C at the current humidity.

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up the Read and Write API keys** — entering the Write key into a `readFloatField` call yields `-999.0` *and zero credit for the function call block*.
> 2. **Forgetting the 20-second delay** — examiners may dock 1 mark for ignoring ThingSpeak's rate-limit policy, as it indicates a lack of *practical* understanding.
> 3. **Casting error** — treating `field1` (a string) directly as a float in a non-library language will silently produce `0.0`; mention the explicit cast in your viva.
> 4. **Channel privacy mismatch** — students often leave the channel *Private* (requiring a key) but build a dashboard without supplying it; the result is HTTP 404 on every request.
> 5. **Not stating units** — writing `28.6` without `°C` or `%RH` costs the "units consistency" half-mark that many model answers reward.
> 6. **Mermaid label formatting** — using `**bold**` inside a quoted Mermaid label is a common KTU lab-record mistake; always use plain uppercase text.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> - ThingSpeak = **cloud IoT platform** supporting 8 numeric fields per channel.
> - **Read API Key** → used in the *retrieval* (GET) operation.
> - **Write API Key** → used in the *upload* (POST) operation.
> - **Channel ID** is a unique integer identifying the channel.
> - The base **read endpoint** is `https://api.thingspeak.com/channels/<id>/feeds.json`.
> - A **single latest feed** is fetched from `/feeds/last.json`.
> - **Results parameter** (`?results=n`) controls the number of historical points returned.
> - All field values arrive as **JSON strings** → cast to `float` before arithmetic.
> - Free-tier **minimum update interval = 15 s** → use `delay(20000)` or longer in firmware.
> - HTTP **200 OK** signals a successful retrieval; **4xx/5xx** indicate errors.
> - **UTC timestamps** are returned; convert to local time (IST = UTC+05:30) if needed.
> - Sentinel value **`-999.0`** from `readFloatField` means a failed read (check key / rate).
> - **DHT11 range**: 0–50 °C, 20–90 %RH. **DHT22 range**: −40 to 80 °C, 0–100 %RH.
> - **Celsius → Fahrenheit**: $T_F = 1.8\,T_C + 32$.
> - **Magnus dew point**: $T_d = \dfrac{b\,\gamma}{a-\gamma}$, $a=17.27$, $b=237.7$.
> - Always **validate bounds** after retrieval to discard sensor glitches and NaN values.
> - The **Read API** is essential for any downstream analytics (dashboards, ML, alerts).
> - Remember the KTU 2024 mantra: **sensors → micro → cloud → retrieve → display**.
